title: Very mixed feeling about this gadget
text:
[[VIDEOID:43080b865b752bd653af5dfe533a6667]] I have very mixed feeling with this smart watch. I have Galaxy Watch 5 and I HAD Apple Watch series 6 previously. Since this was on Vine recommendation list and I just gave it a try. Apps are not stable, problems are here and there but still it's only $30. So I really don't know how to give ratings for this product. So here is what I think.

Build Quality & Design:
It has old Galaxy watch, round shape body with assumably tempered glass. The body is made with aluminum. They give 2 silicone straps gracefully. (Are you seeing this, Samsung & Apple?) For build quality, it's amazingly good for $30, and even without considering the price, it has pretty good quality.

Screen:
They say it's IPS HD resolution but well, it's nothing like major smart watches. Dots are quite visible, probably we are all get used to UHD or QHD level of high resolution screen too much. It's not specifically bothering though, but definitely it's nothing like mainstream watches. It doesn't work as smooth as mainstream watches, then again it's $30.

Comfortableness:
It is pretty comfortable, just like others. I specially like the soft silicone texture of this watch.

Apps:
So, this is the problem. So practically, even with full permission to most of the apps, something works and something doesn't. This watch has connection app and you can manage all your setting there. With full permission, call works pretty good both for dialing and receiving calls. But even with the accessibility permission for messaging, it doesn't show any incoming message nor I can send any, nor does Whatsapp. I'm not sure I'm setting this right or not, but it's true that it's not so convenient. So if someone succeed, please let me know and I will fix rating. The step tracking is somewhat unstable, so it works on and off. The health monitor works pretty well in INCREDABLY SHORT TIME (BUT shows very different number from Galaxy Watch, lol) but blood pressure measure is somewhat unstable, it fluctuates like crazy. I guess it's immature technology since Galaxy Watch 5 didn't open this function for US market yet. So I won't believe whatever number comes up from this watch.

Charger:
It comes with one light plastic charger. Again, it's $30.

Buy it if you need: budget random smart watch for your kids. I mean, it's "working" smart watch for $30.
Don't if you need: you need a real smart watch for messaging and health management. It's just not so credible and reliable.

Last note: I would give 2 star if it were more than $50.

title: Can't afford a Samsung get this!
text: I ended up gtg this watch last year when my Samsung watch stopped working but instead of replacing I ended up waiting for the new one to come out so needed a watch to get me through the month and this watch did an excellent job!  I loved everything about this watch EXCEPT you can't answer or respond to calls/messages!!! AMAZFIT get it together because I seriously would have kept this watch over my new Samsung watch.  It paired with my Zepp scale which was awesome, kept track of all the things I wanted, BP, HR, Blood Oxygen etc. everything I wanted Except the feature to call.  Bummer had to send back.  If they fix that I will prob ditch my Samsung and return to Amazfit.

title: Renewed Galaxy Smartwatch
text:
Refurbished Aspect of this Review:
This isn't my first time ordering a refurbished product from Amazon. I expected the watch to come with the original packaging, unsealed and possibly missing some components inside like inserts and whatnot... Everytime I've ordered Renewed it came in the original packaging... I could get over not having the box fine, (unless I was giving it as a gift.) but what really disappointed me was it didn't include the manual.

It's also important to note that you cannot find the manual in PDF on the Samsung website. They have the manuals for all their other watches, but not this one for some reason. So just keep that in mind.
I still don't know the full functionality of this watch and getting to know it has been a bit frustrating without instructions. You can find review YouTube videos that have some instructions, but you will not be shown everything...

With that out the way, the watch itself is in pristine condition. No scratches or anything. You wouldn't be able to tell it's used. The charger itself did have some wear. The lettering on the back of it is rubbed off in the middle. Not a big deal.

Functionality and Quality Part of this Review:
You know, I bought a cheap end smart watch before getting this one. I didn't get the cheapest one. It was midrange in pricing... I really thought paying so much for a brand name watch is a ripoff before I purchased one. They must work the same when they have similar specs right? NO.
The knockoff watch gave me bizarre heart rate readings. Said I took steps in my sleep, and don't even get me started on the sleep monitor. Be asleep for thirty minutes, wide awake for ten, straight to REM sleep after that for two minutes... It's a joke. The only positive thing I could say about the knockoff is it had 50% better battery life. But again, it was so useless that I wish it would run out of battery.

The body readings on this Galaxy watch are SO accurate that it's a little creepy. Plus it has features that the others don't... Like stress level! Bruh.
This was well worth the money I am very happy with how accurate it is!

One thing that surprises me, and is a bit disappointing is you cannot use the watch to answer calls when you are strictly using Bluetooth and wifi. You need to use a cell service which means you need to pay your mobile carrier more monthly to use the calling feature. With the knockoff you can just use Bluetooth. It sets your watch like a speaker or headphones so you can answer the call...
Same for texting. When I get a text it notifies me but to answer it I have to use my phone.
When someone calls me my watch doesn't ring or tell me that someone is calling. I only get a notification when I miss the call.

It's possible I am just doing something wrong as I don't have the manual... But just some stuff I wanted to share that I would've found helpful!

title: good watch but
text:
This seems to be a common thing among WearOS watches (and Garmin). They squeeze too much into a form factor that's not ready for all of that yet. Remember early Android or iOS? You look at one of those old phones now and wonder, "How the heck did I ever think this was a nice piece of technology?". I feel like that's about where smartwatches are today.

This watch is probably one of the frontrunners (I haven't tried an Apple Watch). It definitely feels more like a smartphone than a fitness tracker. Again, this is due to WearOS. Play Store on-board, you can OK Google and chat and text and call right from the watch (mirrored off your phone). Tons of watch faces, free and paid. Some faces are super customizable, basically changing the UI of the watch (Extra menus/settings/shortcuts/toggles). It gets deep.

But all of this comes at the cost of battery. This isn't the watch's fault, it's the user's. Here's the thing - the native UI is somewhat lacking. The 2nd button has one customizable action - single-press. Holding that button opens Google Pay, and it can;t be changed. No options for 2x press. The other button does default things, but also without a 2x press option. Quick-settings have a set number of toggles, someof which are not what i need at my fingertips but without the option to change. So, a customizable watchface will give me that, but will drain battery. So to get functionality which isn;t limiting to me, I have to pay with juice.

But OK, that's quibbling. Let's talk defaults. The default has multiple fitness apps. Google Fit, as well as the Mobvoi stuff. It's kind of confusing at first, and I set 2 apps to track activity, thus double-draining the battery. That was rough. but then i decided to not use the Mobvoi apps and go with Fit (more activities and it will sync across fitness devices when you change to a different manufacturer). This experience could have been better.

The watch itself is really nice. It has an update out of the box that runs while you're going through the "welcome" stuff so the watch was really sluggish out of the box. I deduced this, but i feel like my parents would be really put off. Again, diminished first experience.

Once you get all of that out of the way, it's really nice. I am still a little confused about when the watch decides to turn the face off on its own, I couldn't find the setting to change the screen timeout. Maybe in the watchface settings, i didn't try there. I feel like this should be in "Display". other than that, it doe all the things I wanted, and then some. Being able to get notifications and reply via wifi when my phone is in the other rooms is nice for my needs. This requires the watch to be on wifi though, and it will destroy your battery in less than a day.

But, with WiFi off, along with the LCD screen feature, the battery should last me over a day, which is enough. It also has a complete saver mode, where the watch goes into just-LCD mode, and WearOS isn;t running, and the watch isn;t doing anything except being a watch. This could last days (they say weeks). My only issue with this mode is that there's no simple backlight (like in a Timex or Casio). Low-power light. The face otherwise isn't visible unless you shioe a light on it. Kinda rough if you wanted to put it into that mode overnight. It's good for outdoors battery-saving, but not for all-around use. They're almost there.

My ultimate issue is that this watch looks a little ridiculous on my smaller wrist. It's huge. it was also unable to read my heartate unless i had it too tight on my wrist in a spot where it was uncomfortable. This isn';t just this watch, same thing with garmin.

I keep trying watches but the bang for the buck doesn't seem to be there for me. Either terrible battery, sluggish, lack of features, or uncomfortable. The one really only has the comfort issue for me, due to weight, size, and desired HR position. I think my best experience so far was with the Samsung Gear Fit 2 (it's like a wide band), but that one couldn't Spotify very well - and the Pro model that does drops NFC. So I figured this T-Pro would do the trick, having both clearly available, but the comfort thing got me.

If you have a larger wrist and are used to wearing bulky watches you probably won't mind it. it's a solid piece of equipment other than being a poor fit for me. Bet battery life i've seen in a WearOS feature-rich watch.

The band it comes with is nice but I don't think it sill be nice for long. it's silicone but with an inlay of leather on the outside, making it look like a leather strap. Problem is it's thin [p]leather, and was wrinkled by the time I took it off/on a couple of times. I would consider the cost of an additional band when you're cost-comparing. I would have been fine with the silicone-only.

I guess I'm saying I'd like this exact watch and all of its functionality in a thinner and lighter form factor. I'd also like some more customizability to WearOS (not more features - jut be able to relocate and remap existing functions). I think we are a few years out from that target, and I'm hoping that manufacturers won't continue to add more functions as the batteries get smaller, then increasing battery size to meet those functions, thereby keeping the watches the same size they are right now. I think the Versa 2 may be the largest i would go, but my experience with their OS is lackluster (thought hey have good tracking). Samsusng's app store reminds me of Samsung "smartphones" pre-iPhone - lack of development and ongoing support of existing apps.

IDK. Gonna keep looking in the band form-factor but I'm giving up for a while in this form factor. Really nice device and I didn't feel ripped off for the price (like with some Samsung products). You won't hate it.

title: Best smartwatch on the market for non Apple users
text:
The main thing I was concerned with was battery life having tried a previous generation Samsung watch that struggled to get 18-24 hours on a charge. What I didn't want was a watch that needed to be charged every night while sleeping and only minimal need to charge during the day. This watch reaches the claimed run time and have yet to run out of power when taking advantage of the time spent getting ready for work. 30 mins of charge time adds about 20% to the watch which is more than enough to get me through the day. I only need to charge to full every other day or two with this strategy. This is with light usage so YMMV. I have the watch set to always on display and touch screen to wake up instead of gesture to limit accidental triggering. Also have GPS off unless needed to further stretch battery life. I am not much for monitoring exercise so those features don't interest me and I can't comment on how well they work.

I primarily use this for sleep monitoring, heart rate, step counting, phone and text messaging, and weather.

Phone calls are clear enough to be heard from the watch itself and the end user says I sound good on their end. I did have some issues trying to get the text settings just right so I would get notifications but got them working eventually. Samsung needs to simplify the process of setting this feature up. There too many not so obvious settings on the phone that can interfere.

Music playing.....I don't recommend this if sound quality is important to you as the audio over bluetooth is flat. Best to use the phone for this if it is nearby. Also using bluetooth audio drains the battery quickly. Having Samsung headphones/buds really helps if you own a pair. The Galaxy Wearable app automatically passes connection info to the watch for instant painless pairing. The wireless headphone function is best used for phone calling when you want more privacy.

Watch face choices....boy are there a lot! Hundreds of watch faces to choose from. A good amount are free, but most are 1-4 dollars. Not sure if it worth paying for a face especially since there doesn't appear to be a way to try them out first before buying.

Apps....again there are many to choose from to add more functionality and information at your fingertips.

The speaker can be set quite loud so no problems missing audible alerts.

Samsung is still making the user experience better, but it is still not a perfect product. I would love a way to visit the Samsung store for watch customization on a laptop or tablet to make browsing easier. Phone screens are just a bit too small for ease of navigation.

title: Worth the money and then some.
text:
I bought this for my mother for Christmas. She would kill me if I spent the money it costs to get an apple watch or samsung gear and such. I figured I would get her a budget one or something. After doing research I found so many "fake" watches.
Then I found this one. Real AMOLED display? Cool. All the reviews were very positive. So Christmas day she opens it.
She called me last night to tell me how much she loves it. She uses it for very simple reasons and that is for fitness and to you know....tell time. She loves it connected to her phone because a couple times a week they walk (her woman's group) and they try for 3 miles. It audibly tells her when she hits each mile and how they did it (with being paired with the phone). She also wears it to bed and says she feels it is accurate about the sleep tracker.
She goes fishing with my dad late at night and they are super pro about it so "no lights!" So the other night they went out for the first time with the watch and she loved it because she could easily see the time without making noise or causing bright lights. My mom absolutely loves it.

If you want to make phone calls and read texts maybe it's not for you. want to use it for heart monitoring,l, fitness monitoring, and telling time? buy it. Ill get one for myself too.

For what you pay for it, you win.

title: Refurbished Samsung Gear Fit2 Pro Watch.
text:
I actually wore out my Samsung Gear Fit2 Pro. Replaced the battery and the metal strap I swapped out on it had corroded. Watch wasn't repairable.
When I started searching for a replacement, my heart was broken. Samsung no longer makes this model.
Then I found a refurbished one on Amazon. I just love this watch. I am beyond happy.
My original Samsung watch actually saved my life years ago. The heart rate indicator actually alerted me that I was having a problem. I went to the hospital to get checked out. I was having a heart attack.
I also like the step counter. I love to see when I reach my goals.
Samsung makes a awesome watch. And it links up with my Samsung phone so well.

title: I needed this
text:
I have avoided a smart watch for years and finally decided to give up the fight and get one. I hate the look of the square Apple watches and mostly think they are over-priced and faddish. I wanted something that would give me the look of a traditional watch and after watching a ton of reviews on YouTube, I decided on the Galaxy Watch 5.

I love the watch but hate the band. I am not reviewing the band but it does seem like if they offer you a classy looking watch they could offer something other than the plastic bands.

The setup is extremely easy with the app downloaded on the phone. I have a Samsung phone which may have made it easier and I don't think all the functions work with other Android phones. I love all the features and integration of the numerous health and lifestyle apps. When I am sitting around the house and have my Samsung phone on the table next to me or in another room, it is great to just look at my wrist to keep up with texts and emails instead of having to chase my phone down.

I followed the tips and tricks from a couple of the instructional videos online and find that the battery has adequate life. I charge my phone every nigh so it is no big deal to have the watch off my wrist for an hour or so during the day so it can charge back up. I keep the display in the "Always On" mode except when I go to bed. I then turn that mode off and set the face to a dim display so it doesn't beam in my face when I turn over at night. Even with the Always On mode activated, the display does go to a dimmer mode after a few seconds. Twisting your wrist or using the other functions will bring it back to full brightness. The watch had a full charge on it around 10 AM yesterday and today at 5 PM it is showing 54% of the charge remaining.

Being a retired worker and family room supervisor I didn't wear a watch most days anyway unless I was going out. I am wearing this constantly except when it is charging or I am taking a shower. I find that it is extremely comfortable (except when I had a cheap 3rd party metal band installed on it that cut into my wrist). Even though the silicone band is cheesy. The body of the watch is very light weight and comfortable.

The bottom line...it has every function available that you could ever want or need and it can be dressed up to give it almost any look you like. I went with the brushed aluminum face so I can put a nice metal band on it and wear it to a senior citizen's prom, or put the silicon band back on for a sweaty sports event. (The one complaint I do have is, the spring clip on the band are a real pain to get clipped back if you are into switching bands. It took me about 10 minutes of fiddling this morning to get those tiny pins back in place. Every time I though I had them locked in, when I tugged on the band, I had the watch in one had and half the strap in the other).

It's nice to read all the reviews here, but for the best impression of the watch I found it more useful to watch the evaluation videos online, especially the tips, tricks, and suggested functions that you should install once you power the watch up.

title: I LOVE this camera, but there's one HUGE FLAW!
text:
As a Samsung S7 Edge owner, I love this camera, but it has one MAJOR flaw that the end user needs to be cognitive of at all times, or the camera will be damaged (as mine was/is). I've had the camera for a about a week, and this has been my experience so far.

Out of the Box: Getting started with the Gear 360 2017 was very simple. I un-boxed and charged it, read the quick start guide, downloaded the Samsung Gear 360 app from PlayStore and I was taking photos and shooting video right away. I literally posted my first 360-degree video to Facebook within 10-minutes of connecting the camera to my Android. It is very simple and worked flawlessly. To get a little farther, I watched a couple of quick YouTube videos to learn more about the live streaming functionality, which I may never use, but is still cool if you have internet excellent bandwidth.

Pros: Again, simplicity. With my Samsung S7 Edge Android phone, I was able to snap fun photos and video and post to Facebook with little effort. The photo/video is converted from the dual-fish-eye view to the 360-degree format automatically, so it is literally as simple as uploading. The battery life is very good (held a charge for the entire week with intermittent use), and the Bluetooth connection to the phone worked flawlessly every time. As a 360-degree camera, this is really easy to use. It now works with Android and IOS (newer versions), although the Live Streaming doesn't currently work with IOS, and there may still be a lower resolution in IOS at this time.

Cons: I really didn't have a negative experience with the Gear 360 yet. Your IOS and Android phone just need to be on a compatible OS. The one main flaw was this:
- The Gear 360 is top heavy. It simply falls over if there is the least amount of instability under the base, and the rubber "stabilizer" ring supplied with it is useless (if not detrimental in giving a false sense of security). I literally have placed the camera on a slightly-less-than-horizontal surface, and it is perfectly fine for 30-seconds, then it suddenly falls over like a drunk sailor for no apparent reason. I've scratched both lenses, and here's the next big negative, there's no lens replacement kit for this camera (yet, maybe?). To repair the lens, you must currently ship it back to Korea.

Advice? If you buy this camera, buy a decent little tripod for it and take the tripod with you wherever you go. Throw the "stabilizer ring" in the recycle bin and just use the tripod.

title: It's garbage, but it's really CHEAP garbage.
text:
Understand going in: This watch is utter crap. It's garbage. It's a "smart" watch only inasmuch as it can connect via bluetooth to your phone and make/receive calls from your connected phone, or act as a very limited standalone phone with a SIM card.

However, it's only $10.

For $10, it's actually pretty damn cool. It plays music (albeit on a tiny, practically useless speaker) and can make & receive calls. For ten lousy bucks, what the hell more do you expect? The Samsung Gear 2 smart watch costs $200, and doesn't actually do a whole lot more when you get down to brass tacks. Sure, it's sturdier, and prettier, and has more flexibility; it's also 20x more expensive than this humble little $10 piece of crap that, should it get smashed, you wouldn't waste a nanosecond worrying about.

If you have an extra ten spot burning a hole in your pocket, pick up one of these crappy little watches. They're cute, and kinda fun.

title: Screen is dim and menus are clunky but for the price the Fenix 3 is hard to beat.  Its all about the Garmin Ecosysem!
text:
Just got this after running through several Garmin devices. I started with a Fenix 5. When I pay as much as a Fenix 5 costs I expect to be able to read it. Yes, I'm particular like that. So many people rave about how the Fenix series easy to read in the sun but it just isn't...and its BARELY readable indoors. Even if it did look great outdoors, why would I trade that for not being able to see it the other 15 waking hours of the day? Samsung looks unbelievably good indoors and looks readable in direct sunlight. Poor design Garmin. But for he price you can get this baby for now, I am fine with the display.

Also the interface is pretty clunky. Learn from Samsung. And for those user who say they prefer buttons, who cares... give us a touch screen too to navigate all the ridiculously abstract menus... While I'm at it: who the heck cares what the difference is between an app and a widget? Make themn the same thing dudes...really. Every time you have to access something you have to figure out how to get there. Who wants to work that hard with a WATCH. So I returned my Fenix 5 and bought a Fenix 3 HR Sapphire instead because for less than half the price I can live with the limitations. ...happily. Why?

Garmin's fitness tracking ecosystem is the best out there in my opinion. I have maintained Garmin and Samsung for a few years now and finally sold my Samsung Gear S3 Frontier yesterday.

Garmin:
1) Has a WIFI scale (essential and very convenient) which I use and love
2) Has the Vivofit series which I use for travel because they are tiny and require no battery charging hardware... which I love
3) Syncs with Strava (Samsung does not sync with Strava)
4) Provides lots of quality metrics, equal to Strava for Biking IMO)
5) A great product line of many choices especially for size and price

So after trying out about every product in the Garmin line I settled with a Fenix 3 HR Sapphire, Vivofit 2, Vivosmart HR, and the Index smart scale. and lived happily ever after...

title: Not as nice as a Samung Galaxy watch
text:
I had purchased a new LTE Samsung Galaxy 4 watch, but realized due to my heavily tattooed wrists, the Samsung watch wouldn't work. The sensor in the Samsung watch never thought I was actually wearing it, so it wouldn't send any alerts.

I did some research and came across this Garmin Fenix 6. Sure enough, I receive all alerts when wearing the watch. So far, it hasn't skipped a beat.

But, this watch isn't nearly as nice as a Galaxy watch. The screen on this Fenix isn't a touch screen, and you can't rotate the bezel to move between the apps. This is a push button watch. Everything you want to do, you need to push a button on the side. And the buttons don't feel that nice either. Overall, the watch works. And I'm glad I was able to pick it up on sale, but there's no way this watch is worth over $300. I wouldn't pay that much for it.

The watch is cumbersome, and like I said, it doesn't have the build quality of the Samsung. But guess what? I can't use a Samsung, so here I am. I'm going to keep the watch, and I wear it every day, but I am a little disappointed in the high price for a watch that doesn't reflect that.

I have synched the watch to my phone, but for some of the features, I need to synch it with my laptop. Again, cumbersome.

I was mainly looking for a smart watch that would work even though I have a lot of tattoos on my wrists, and I did find it. But.....if you can wear a Samsung watch, I'd buy that instead.

title: Service beyond expectations
text:
My Samsung Gear Active 2 smartwatch bricked after a little over a year. The manufacturer's warranty was expired by a couple months and so they would not help me at all with replacement. I bought an Asurion protection plan because I'm getting too used to my devices breaking just outside of warranty.

I thought Asurion would have hidden charges, logistical hoops to jump through, and other garbage that I have experienced in the past with insurance plans. Not this! I did not have much luck with the mobile app, so I called a support representative and they were able to get me set up with minimal confusion or hassle and then they got me a replacement device at no extra charge to me. I admit I had low expectations making the purchase, but in the end I was so pleased that I told family and friends about it. Pretty strange to have such a good experience with such low initial expectations.

title: Where is my Pebble Time 2 with HRM?!
text:
Smartwatches have had a difficult journey --especially in 2016. Expected sales have been half of projected, and when billions of revenue dollars and market size are the object of investors this does not bode well. This was made all the more real by apple’s own disappointing apple watch sale figures, and even more depressing Fitbit’s poor fiscal year and necessary consolidation with Pebble (Question: is being bought a symbol of growth or rescue?).

Admittedly, watches in general have become less of a necessity with certain younger demographics tied to their cell phones and less conscious of what is on their wrist, whether utilitarian, nerdy, or fashion trendy. Of course, when we can put in our “smart contact lenses” and experience as much “overlay”, interaction, and connectivity we might desire, without looking down at anything, in the privacy of our own world and outside of the transitory “google glass” solution, the beginning of the end of smartphones is inevitable! I, for one, look forward to this Terminator style overlay, but with less red coloration, and more “Minority Report” interactivity.

So the smart watch industry continues to try to make inroads into the same demographic that might not want a watch, but would want a device that gives an active, youthful, and free feeling—more dynamic less utilitarian. “Active” is the sense that actual activity is truly measured and quantified. “Free feeling” in that you do not need to carry around your phone everywhere you go or more simply, that you do not need to look at it to acknowledge messages or phone calls (voice, how quaint!). For apple, the do it all smart phone substitute is their watch. The apple watch is nice. The screen is bright, application rich, and reportedly provides good accuracy during activities. I would not know, I do not have one. I do not want one until the battery life improves significantly. What good is an activity watch that you cannot wear continuously for at least 24 hours to track activity and sleep? That, and the price… oh lordy, the price of entry is high, even for the base models.

Enough commentary! For some background, I have/had a number of these devices.

Way back in the old days, I had a Polar watch with a chest band. These devices were (and continue to be) the go to items for accurate HRM during activity. Long on battery life, but way short on apps and connectivity in those days.

Then there was an Epson product that seemed out of their normal printer based business model. It was a basic band providing a couple of lights showing and tracking activity with a heart rate monitor (hereafter, HRM) and requiring connection to your phone for exercise tracking. It provided inconsistent HR data during certain activities (for me, stationary bike riding showed wildly inaccurate measurements), but did seem to provide interesting sleep information.

Then, a basic and early Fitbit band, without HRM, which worked OK for tracking but in the end I needed more display—why wear a watch and a band with no HRM? The variable reviews for the higher end HRM Fitbit did not prompt me to purchase one, along with a lack of an app universe and less than exciting displays.

That led to a Pebble. Notably a “Pebble Time” purchased during a kickstarter campaign. The Pebble Time has an always on color display, that only looks better outside in bright sunlight (less so inside), has pretty good 3-5 days of battery life, a great app and developer space, with acceptable activity tracking (minus HRM) and OK looks. If Pebble had lasted and carried through with their kickstarter project to provide a model like this with HRM, I would have purchased one. But no, about the same time that Pebble was supposed to carry through with that product, Fitbit bought them up –but not for their hardware acumen, but to give Fitbit a necessary app and developer community. Fitbit has not provided any solid hints they will provide a similar device to the one that Pebble did not deliver. (I do love my Pebble, and do know that the Pebble 2 with HRM is probably a great watch—but alas, no color? And no future?!).

That finally brings me to what I have realized is most important to me in any of these devices. I want a clear, colorful, and configurable display. The variety and changeability of watch faces was my initial and continuous draw to any of these watches. It needs to be accurate, and I do want HRM. It needs a proper interface on a larger screen for control and setup. It does not need to always be on, but it does need to come on when I need it. Being a Pebble fan, touchscreens were less important, but that may be changing since I got the Samsung Fit2. (Finally, he is talking about the Samsung!?).

The Samsung Gear devices, and notably the Fit2 had been on my list. I was waiting for Gear to provide apple iOS support outside of the android phones AND acceptable battery life. I was already familiar with Samsung’s gear community and my primary need for configurable watch faces. Samsung checked off iOS support in February 2017 (finally!) providing a basic app for iPhone. I started watching for Amazon Warehouse open box deals since I did not feel like spending full price on yet another disappointment. When a 50% off deal showed up (with acceptable condition notes), I jumped.

I have had the Fit2 for about a week now. Maybe not a lot of time to comment, but still:
What I like:
*Super bright display under most conditions (see below)
*Enough available watch faces to make me happy
*Screen that does seem to come on when I want it
*Good accuracy for HRM, in general and during exercise compared to my exercise bike monitor.
*The iOS app works (see below)
*The Samsung developer app is pretty nice for watch face and apps (but see below)
*I like having a touchscreen!
*The on watch touch and button based interface is mostly intuitive.

What I do not like:
*The Samsung iOS app is very basic and needs much work compared to their own android based app (and compared to the Pebble OS app).
**Additionally the iOS app is not fully implemented, not providing expected functionality across the entire Samsung GearApp marketplace. Looking for Broda or Infinity watch faces? No access provided, and the providers say directly that Samsung must provide that full access? That is very poor implementation. Come on Samsung, allow iOS users access to the full market place.
*In same area, the health tracking application needs more work with more detailed and configurable (maybe downloadable) datasets, along with potential interactivity with other apps like “my fitness pal”.
*The display is harder to read in direct sunlight, and the main display cannot be set to “always on”. (You can see an always on default basic hour:min lower power display if you choose). I need to be able to time certain operations for short periods of time over a minute but less than 10 minutes with my main face, and without flicking my wrist to bring my watch back up.
*Needs a wider app space, with yet more watch faces AND the ability for a user to create their own watch faces in the developer app without needing to register for the Samsung marketplace and developer space (I just want to create watch faces and upload them to my watch without putting them up on the Samsung GalaxyApps seller space. And so far, Samsung has been non-responsive for my own submissions—stalled in “registration” status. What gives?).

What I am neutral on:
*The looks. It is less watch and more sports band.
*Battery life is average, with my use, at best 2-3 days.
*Perceived durability. Everything seems solid so far, but I am wary based on surrounding reviews.

What I would recommend:
Buy one—even at the retail price, it seems a good value! Even more so if you can find an acceptable open box from Amazon Warehouse to try. You may like it, especially if you are looking for an alternative to Fitbit or apple devices.
Also buy a screen protector with your Fit2 like [[ASIN:B01G4HI3ZQ Samsung Gear Fit2 Screen Protector, IQ Shield LiQuidSkin (6-Pack) Full Coverage Screen Protector for Samsung Gear Fit2 (Gear Fit 2) HD Clear Anti-Bubble Film - with]]...There are a number of examples of cracked screens out there--protect your watch from the beginning.

What do I really want? My color Pebble Time 2 with HRM! Oh well. 4/5.

Expected sales have been half of projected, and when billions of revenue dollars and market size are the object of investors this does not bode well. title: Magnificent with a few frustrations
text:
These Sony wireless ear buds are my favorite tech item. I absolutely love so much about them from the sound quality to the compactness to the battery life. But they are flawed enough that I do not think they deserve five stars. 9 out of 10 if it was possible.

How can this be? Read on.

(Programming note: I tested these headphones with a useful Spotify playlist called "Songs to Test Headphones With" -- I also listened to my regular music for months and months with these headphones because I enjoy them so much and because it was so hard for me to write this review)

Sound quality:

Everything begins with the sound quality. The Sony WH1000XM3 over ear headphones have this magical, immersive sound that makes you rediscover music and luxuriate in sound like nothing I've experienced before, despite having listened to some very good mid-range headphones before. The problem with the WH1000XM3 headphones is the size. They are large over the ear headphones. That is what makes the WF-1000xM3 earbuds so incredible. Sony engineers have somehow managed to get 85% of the sound quality of the WH1000xM3 headphones into small ear buds.

If sound quality is important to you and you need earbuds you can stuff in a pocket, these earbuds are absolutely exceptional. If you travel everywhere with a bag and this is not an issue, consider the WH1000xM3 over ear phones.

There is absolutely no comparison to lesser earbuds like the Samsung Gear Icon X--the Icon X sound like scratchy AM radio after listening to music on the Sony earbuds.

Bluetooth phone calls:

The quality of bluetooth calls leaves a lot to be desired. In a quiet place, sure its fine. But in any kind of noisy place it is almost impossible to have a call. The aforementioned Gear Icon X do a better job. Sony seems to struggle with bluetooth generally.

Bluetooth connection:

The earbuds independently connect to bluetooth, which is great, but I suspect they have some issues in terms of bandwidth/transmission, because they seem to work flawlessly in my home but out and about they frequently drop. Perhaps a more technically minded person has more insights.

Battery life:

Absolutely stunning. Not only have I never run out of charge on the battery case despite being tremendously lazy about charging it, but I've never run out of charge on the buds. This would doubtless change if I wore them on an international flight, but for ordinary daily use, you'll be hard pressed to have battery issues.

Fit and comfort: I don't love the fit of these buds. They are a little bigger than the typical earbud and always feel a little awkward when you are walking around. It's important to find a positive fit for both noise cancelling and sound quality, so this may take you a little work. They also are not great earbuds to work out in as they don't tolerate sweat well. That said: they are without a doubt the most comfortable earbuds for long duration listening I've ever used. I even fall asleep wearing these earbuds and wake up hours later with no discomfort. Really exceptional.

Noise cancelling: Actually really fantastic BUT Sony has insisted on making the default settings less than full noise cancelling, maybe because they are worried about people walking around and getting run down by cars they don't hear. To take life into your own hands, you have to go into the Sony app and set everything to max. Once you do, the outside world is very effectively blocked out. Yes, you can still hear murmurs, but it knocks everything down twenty decibels. This is amazing for plane rides and other persistently noisy environments. The only problem is that people cannot see the earbuds in your ears and sometimes will end up yelling at you, frustrated that you cannot hear them.

About that app:

It's a little finicky but absolutely essential to gain control over your earbuds. If I wasn't able to turn off the touch controls on the side of the earbuds, they would be a 2 star product for me. But because I am able to go into the app and control this, the earbuds are much more useful.

Ditto some other quirks: if you pull one ear bud out while on a call, the other ear bud persists. If you pull one ear bud out while listening to music it pauses. You can change this in the app as well. I am not sure which way I prefer, but its nice to have the option.

You can also let the app analyze your ear shape for a supposedly better quality sound. I haven't gotten around to doing this.

If you drop one of your earbuds, Sony has your back by providing a blinking light. This light is only active if the earbud doesn't sense any pressure around it (ie its not in your fingers or your ear). This is pretty clever.

Missed opportunities: I do not know why manufacturers insist on putting controls on ear buds. I never use them in a positive fashion and I just end up mistakenly doing things while adjusting my earbuds. Instead, why not make the battery case a remote control? I would also love to see a special edition of the case decked out like a Sony Walkman. Wouldn't that be cool?

Overall, I think these earbuds are fantastic. You get truly exceptional sound quality--a balanced and immersive experience that sounds great for everything from classical music to hip hop and rock in between, truly hearing all the details of your music--in a package that fits into a jeans pocket. That's pretty amazing. Add to that very good noise cancelling and excellent battery life. True, the bluetooth calling leaves a lot to be desired and occasional bluetooth drops may frustrate you, but pound for pound, these earbuds are the champs.

Recommended!

in my home but out and about they frequently drop perhaps a more technically minded person has more insights battery life absolutely stunning not only have i never run out of charge on the battery case despite being tremendously lazy about charging it but i ve never run out of charge on the buds this would doubtless change if i wore them on an international flight but for ordinary daily use you ll be hard pressed to have battery issues fit and comfort i don t love the fit of these buds they are a little bigger than the typical earbud and always feel a little awkward when you are walking around it s important to find a positive fit for both noise cancelling and sound quality so this may take you a little work they also are not great earbuds to work out in as they don t tolerate sweat well that said they are without a doubt the most comfortable earbuds for long duration listening i ve ever used i even fall asleep wearing these earbuds and wake up hours later with no discomfort really exceptional noise cancelling actually really fantastic but sony has insisted on making the default settings less than full noise cancelling maybe because they are worried about people walking around and getting run down by cars they don t hear to take life into your own hands you have to go into the sony app and set everything to max once you do the outside world is very effectively blocked out yes you can still hear murmurs but it knocks everything down twenty decibels this is amazing for plane rides and other persistently noisy environments the only problem is that people cannot see the earbuds in your ears and sometimes will end up yelling at you frustrated that you cannot hear them about that app it s a little finicky but absolutely essential to gain control over your earbuds if i wasn t able to turn off the touch controls on the side of the earbuds they would be a star product for me but because i am able to go into the app and control this the earbuds are much more useful ditto some other quirks if you pull one ear bud out while on a call the other ear bud persists if you pull one ear bud out while listening to music it pauses you can change this in the app as well i am not sure which way i prefer but its nice to have the option you can also let the app analyze your ear shape for a supposedly better quality sound i haven t gotten around to doing this if you drop one of your earbuds sony has your back by providing a blinking light this light is only active if the earbud doesn t sense any pressure around it ie its not in your fingers or your ear this is pretty clever missed opportunities i do not know why manufacturers insist on putting controls on ear buds i never use them in a positive fashion and i just end up mistakenly doing things while adjusting my earbuds instead why not make the battery case a remote control i would also love to see a special edition of the case decked out like a sony walkman wouldn t that be cool overall i think these earbuds are fantastic you get truly exceptional sound quality a balanced and immersive experience that sounds great for everything from classical music to hip hop and rock in between truly hearing all the details of your music in a package that fits into a jeans pocket that s pretty amazing add to that very good noise cancelling and excellent battery life true the bluetooth calling leaves a lot to be desired and occasional bluetooth drops may frustrate you but pound for pound these earbuds are the champs recommended

title: A review from the perspective of a long-time Apple Watch user
text:
I've recently transitioned from all-Apple devices to the Android ecosystem. One of my biggest "worries" about this transition was getting a replacement for my Apple Watch, as my impression of Android-based wearables wasn't positive. While the Apple Watch is by no means perfect, the user experience is, generally, excellent, and the build quality of the Apple Watch is outstanding. After doing quite a bit of research for an Android smartwatch, most reviewers seem to recommend this model, the Galaxy Watch 4, or the Fossil Gen 5 Smartwatch as the clearest front-runners for best Wear OS devices available at the moment. For perspective, my use-case for a smartwatch is to be able to tell time, get notifications, and do some degree of fitness tracking, though the latter is relatively less important to me. I ultimately went with the TicWatch because of the crazy advertised battery life and my strong aversion to getting sucked in to Samsung's own ecosystem within Android, which seemed to be necessary to get all of the functionality out of the Galaxy Watch without having to do some hacking that I wasn't interested in doing. Some of the reviews on Amazon and elsewhere had me quite worried about this device. After daily-driving it for nearly a month, though, I'm EXTREMELY impressed and very happy with the purchase. If you have similar trepidation, read on and buy without regret.

THE GOOD:
- I think the build quality of the TicWatch is actually quite good. Yes, the bulk of the watch body is plastic, but it's a solid, high-quality plastic that looks and feels like a premium device. I really haven't noticed missing the all-glass-and-metal design of the Apple Watch. Some reviews derided the TicWatch as looking like a "toy" because of the plastic body, which I absolutely disagree with. Despite the $300 price point, I'm not at all disappointed with the build quality. It's not as sleek as the Apple Watch, but the TicWatch is significantly lighter, and the beautiful chromed surface of the Apple Watch eventually gets scratched to hell without some kind of protective case. In contrast, I haven't had that experience with the TicWatch so far. The steel bezel seems to resist scratches well, and I haven't noticed any blemishes on my unit so far despite wearing it nearly 24 hours/day.

- The battery life on the TicWatch is INCREDIBLE. This is really a total game changer compared to the Apple Watch, which had an awful battery life that required daily charging. On the TicWatch, I have all monitoring enabled, all notifications enabled, and am using the default screen options (keeping the primary AMOLED display on for only 5 seconds after the last interaction and using the LCD display at all other times except when a new notification comes in). With this setup, I run the battery down by 30-40% each day. Right now, for example, I last charged the TicWatch to 100% ~30 hours ago and still have 67% charge remaining. At this rate, 2-3 days of battery life is no problem, and daily charging is no longer a necessity. I will say that the charger kind of sucks - more on that below - but the battery life is outstanding.

- I love the dual-display setup on the TicWatch. I do wish that the LCD had a bit more functionality - for example, it'd be nice if there was an indication that there are unread notifications and displayed a pulse reading - and had some degree of customization options (which I know would be limited by the technology), but it's otherwise outstanding. The backlight looks exactly like a classic, "old-school" LCD watch - think Timex's Indiglo light. If you need to get to the AMOLED display, a push of one of the crowns gets you there. This was a genius decision by Mobvoi, and the battery life improvements make this totally worthwhile and something other smartwatch makers should take notice of.

- Performance on the TicWatch is excellent. Screen transitions, animations, and opening apps are all snappy. The experience doesn't feel at all janky, and it's largely comparable to my experience with the Apple Watch. If, like me, you're worried about how this device will perform, don't be - the user experience is excellent.

- One of the common complaints that I read about the TicWatch was having to rely on the touchscreen for all input - in other words, the lack of rotating bezel or crown to scroll the screen. I actually haven't found this to be an issue in day-to-day use. Sure, a functional crown would be nice, but the screen is large enough that scrolling on the screen directly isn't difficult. At this price point the lack of a scrolling crown does seem odd, but I don't think the use of the device is hampered in anyway by its absence. YMMV.

- As a recent immigrant from Apple devices, I've actually found the Wear OS experience to be decent (at the time of this review, Wear OS 3 isn't available). I do think Apple's UI is superior and easier to use, but I don't find the Wear OS experience to be so absolutely lacking or terrible as to be a problem. YMMV, I suppose, as I know Wear OS is frequently lamented as being neglected by Google. As someone new to the Android ecosystem, this isn't evident to me.

THE MEH:
- To get all of the functionality out of this device, you'll need to use Mobvoi's apps. I will say that basic fitness readings and other data collected by Mobvoi's apps do seem to integrate pretty well with Google Fit, which I prefer to use. Google Fit doesn't collect everything, bit it's good enough for me: pulse readings, sleep time, and step counts. For anything else, I use a shortcut on the watch face for Mobvoi's apps so that I can check specific parameters if I want (like the pseudoscientific "stress" readings). Unlike other reviewers, I haven't had any issues with inconsistent step counts - everything is more or less consistent across the watch, Google Fit, my calorie tracking app, and Mobvoi's apps, though it does take a bit of time for the data to be pushed around. That said, Mobvoi's apps need some work. They clearly look and feel like poorly translated apps with a design language that seems out of place to my American eye. The data in the Mobvoi apps are slow to update, even when forced to do so. I don't frequently use the Mobvoi apps since I prefer Google's interface, but the suboptimal experience here is unfortunate.

- The charger needs some work. It works well most of the time, but like some reviewers have mentioned, I've occasionally had issues with the watch presumably being connected to the charger but not actually charging. I've gotten into the habit of checking to make sure that the watch indicates that it's charging before leaving it to charge. Charging is also slower than I'd like, as many reviews have mentioned - it takes about an hour to top off the watch after being used for a 24-hour period. I suppose that's not terrible, but it seems a lot slower than the Apple Watch, which can nearly fully charge in that period of time. The absence of magnetic charging is a bummer - I'm not a fan of the cable-based charging, which reminds me of my Pebble watch that I had back in the day. I hope that the next model will improve on this point.

THE BAD:
- The only negative point included in most reviews of this device that I actually agree with is that the haptic motor sucks. The motor is totally garbage, and I can't believe that Mobvoi presumably tested this device and stuck with the motor anyway. As many other reviewers have mentioned, I hear the haptic motor more than I feel it. This absolutely needs to be fixed in the next model - at this price point, the inclusion of this garbage motor is really inexcusable.

In summary, I'm generally quite happy with the TicWatch, and I have no regrets about the purchase. I think it's fairly priced for what you get, the experience has, in general, been solid and comparable to my experience with the Apple Watch, and it performs exactly as I would hope a smartwatch would. It's not a perfect device, but the quirks and annoyances don't actively interfere with its usability, and none of them are deal-breakers for me. If, like me, you like the idea of the insane battery life but are worried about some of the negative reviews you may have read, take the plunge and go for it. I've been surprised by how positive the experience has been, and I'm a believer in Mobvoi now. Highly, highly recommended!

title: Great for biking and some activities, but the built-in optical heart rate monitor disappoints -- as does it size and weight
text:
If you want a fitness tracker, and want to track your sleep, etc., and do some light exercising infrequently (like twice in January and then give up), this is NOT your watch. Go get an Apple Watch, or a Samsung Gear, and be happy. On the other hand, if you're a fitness freak and really want the details on your workouts, this *might* be for you. Read on.

OK, still here, good. What this watch is good at: Great barometer, GPS, data tracking, external sensor flexibility, and fit/finish. What it's just OK at: user interface and web experience. And the ugly: the built-in heart rate monitor.

OK, there's a ton behind that last paragraph. I'm an avid sports electronics nut, and have a variety of Garmin and other brand sport watches, bike computers, power meters, etc., etc. I have to charge ELEVEN things at the end of a bike ride. I'll try and distill two weeks of hardcore testing down to a few paragraphs.

The best experience I've had with the Suunto is while riding a bike. It pairs with my bike power meter (unlike Garmin which arbitrarily hobbles all but their top end watches from this basic functionality). It pairs with my [[ASIN:B00JQHTJS2 Heart Rate Monitor]]. It pairs with my bike's speed/candence sensors. It just bloody well works, and works brilliantly.

It's also great for hill walking. The barometer is really top rate. And the mapping function is really great, too. It's too big for running, and I'm a big guy (195 lbs/88 kg, 6' / 183 cm)... I'm slow enough as it is; I don't need further hindrances!

The worst experience: using it as a fitness tracker, or tracking heart rate during the day or especially at night. The thing is just too darned heavy, the built-in optical HRM doesn't work for me (I think it's a function of weight; the heaver the device the more it's affected by movement and the harder it is to separate said movement from heart rate measurement), and Suunto's app and website don't really let you get at past data in any decent way. Oh, yeah, and unlike my [[ASIN:B0711LKZKX Nokia Steel HR]] or [[ASIN:B074KBWL9J Vívoactive 3]], it's BIG and UNCOMFORTABLE while sleeping.

More on the heart rate issue: it would often report an insanely high heart rate (like 160 bpm) when my real heart rate was 110. It seemed to be reporting a mashup of cadence, actual heart rate, arm swinging, whatever... It would settle down and get accurate, and then go crazy again. Other reviewers report similar issues, and one reports that after a few weeks of "getting to know him" it settled down. Mine hasn't settled down. I just wear my Scosche when exercising with the watch. The built-in HRM is useless. Bah.

The first two pictures show a size comparison between the Garmin, the Suunto, and the Scosche HRM. "One of these isn't like the other" and is way too darned big!

The UI is a bit fiddly. My top complaint is font size. For such a big watch, it tries to cram too much info on a screen – and as a result uses a font too small for me to read easily, e.g. when I get a notification of an e-mail or text message. The font is ESPECIALLY bad when I'm running, etc. I don't need to read a novel; just give me the basics and I'll be happy!

Oh, and I had a *terrible* initial experience with the watch. It bricked itself about an hour into my use of it. I had to send it back for repair. The good news is that the person on the phone was completely gracious about it, and within seconds I was set up with a paid UPS sticker to return it. The bad news was that it took a few weeks to get it back (OK, it was over New Year's, but still...). No real info other than "reloaded software."

Why four stars then? Because for the use cases where it works, it's GREAT, Simply GREAT. It's just not a Swiss Army Knife (or even a Finnish Army Knife :-) like the Garmin watches. I'm glad I own it, but the Garmin is still my "daily driver" activity watch. If you're looking for a great watch for biking (and you have a power meter), walking and like big ("I'm important") watches, then it's five stars, easy.

Disclosure 1: I haven't taken it swimming yet. I plan to, but it's January and even in San Diego the water is a bit chilly.

Disclosure 2: I work for a sister company to one that makes smart watches. I don't work with this folks, and am writing this review as an individual.

The thing is just too darned heavy, the built-in optical HRM doesn't work for me (I think it's a function of weight; the heaver the device the more it's affected by movement and the harder it is to separate said movement from heart rate measurement), and Suunto's app and website don't really let you get at past data in any decent way. Oh, yeah, and unlike my [[ASIN:B0711LKZKX Nokia Steel HR]] or [[ASIN:B074KBWL9J Vívoactive 3]], it's BIG and UNCOMFORTABLE while sleeping.

More on the heart rate issue: it would often report an insanely high heart rate (like 160 bpm) when my real heart rate was 110. It seemed to be reporting a mashup of cadence, actual heart rate, arm swinging, whatever... It would settle down and get accurate, and then go crazy again. Other reviewers report similar issues, and one reports that after a few weeks of "getting to know him" it settled down. Mine hasn't settled down. I just wear my Scosche when exercising with the watch. The built-in HRM is useless. Bah.

The first two pictures show a size comparison between the Garmin, the Suunto, and the Scosche HRM. "One of these isn't like the other" and is way too darned big!

The UI is a bit fiddly. My top complaint is font size. For such a big watch, it tries to cram too much info on a screen – and as a result uses a font too small for me to read easily, e.g. when I get a notification of an e-mail or text message. The font is ESPECIALLY bad when I'm running, etc. I don't need to read a novel; just give me the basics and I'll be happy!

Oh, and I had a *terrible* initial experience with the watch. It bricked itself about an hour into my use of it. I had to send it back for repair. The good news is that the person on the phone was completely gracious about it, and within seconds I was set up with a paid UPS sticker to return it. The bad news was that it took a few weeks to get it back (OK, it was over New Year's, but still...). No real info other than "reloaded software."

Why four stars then? Because for the use cases where it works, it's GREAT, Simply GREAT. It's just not a Swiss Army Knife (or even a Finnish Army Knife :-) like the Garmin watches. I'm glad I own it, but the Garmin is still my "daily driver" activity watch. If you're looking for a great watch for biking (and you have a power meter), walking and like big ("I'm important") watches, then it's five stars, easy.

Disclosure 1: I haven't taken it swimming yet. I plan to, but it's January and even in San Diego the water is a bit chilly.

Disclosure 2: I work for a sister company to one that makes smart watches. I don't work with this folks, and am writing this review as an individual.

title: Good Value
text: Great value for the money. It does not track sleeping very well at all, at least not for me who works overnight. I have the option to manually edit the amount of sleep but I don't understand the point of doing it when it won't be able to tell me if it was deep sleep or any other details. It literally shows me as being up for weeks at a time even though I sleep during the day. Good for workouts and simple functions such reading text messages or seeing who's calling without taking it my phone. Battery life is insane and I still love it and wear it ahead of my Samsung watch.

title: Not so stupid, I am teachable - I am so STUPID!
text:
Uodate 2/09/2027
The picture below shows my easy and cheap fix for the watchband. I just did not want to buy a new wrist band that might or might not fit my tracker. I bought 20 ponytail/braid bands at the drugstore for $2.00. I doubled one over the band and it holds my band snugly and is easy to get off and on.
Update 2/05/2017
The snow has melted quite a bit, and I found my Gearfit 2 just inside the pasture fence where I throw hay to the horses. Fortunately, my 1,200 pound Tennessee Walker had not stepped on it. I brought it up to the house, put it on the charger and reconnected it to the Samsung Gear app my phone. So, after 8 days of being buried in the snow with night temps in the upper teens, my Gearfit 2 is working just fine. I am going to order a different band for it, but until it comes, I have taken a bread bag twisty tie and secured my band. Samsung needs to fix this issue and not rely on the cottage industry to pick up the slack. So many people are not as lucky as I was today to find their watches.
Now on to performance. I do really like this tracker and maybe because I live out in the country, I am more tolerant of glitchy electronics and intermittent internet connections. #satellitemegalagtimes #thankyouelectriccoopforrunningfiberoptics The issues I have with the tracker are: 1. it does not register my stair climbing faithfully. Sometimes it does the house stairs. Sometimes it doesn't. It almost always gets the elevation change outside from house to barn - equivalent to two flights of stairs. 2. The step count on the Gearfit 2 does not match up with steps on my Samsung Galaxy S5. The phone always shows more steps. 3. The exercise choices do not match up with the phone. I have horsebackriding on my phone app but not on the tracker. The GPS works well out here in the boonies. I like the many options in settings. I turn off all notifications because I want to prolong battery life - I go two full days and still have battery - and I really don't want to know who is calling, texting, on FB while I am out walking in my gorgeous frozen northland or asleep. Yes, it has a few issues and I can deal with the ball park number of steps and stairs. It is good for my moderate OCD to not have things perfect. I am grateful that I found it right where my husband said it would probably be. <3 Thank you St. Anthony. ;)

1/29/2017 Man, oh man am I angry and do I ever feel stupid! I researched the different types of fitness watch/monitors carefully. I have probably been looking at different ones for a year now. Everything about the GearFit 2 was great. I liked the look, the options, the fact that I didn't have to crane my neck and wrist around to read the data. It synced beautifully with my Samsung Galaxy phone. It kept accurate steps, sleep, heart beat. In a word, it was FANTASTIC. You may be wondering why I gave it one star. IT'S THE WRIST BAND!! My lovely easy to use fantastic fitness aid is now out in the horse barn, hay barn or under 3 inches of new fallen snow. I read the reviews and the warnings about the watch falling off people's wrists and disappearing. I also read that Samsung had improved it with some type of keeper to prevent that. There is a loop that you pass the band through before fastening it into its slot. IF THAT WAS THE FIX, IT AINT' ENOUGH!!! My lovely, I-told-you-so applefitbit friends will certainly get a charge out of this if they find out. I don't care what people say, I'm just really really angry and feel that I have been cheated by Samsung. Most of their products are good, but I will not be buying another GearFit2 or any other GearFit. They are too expensive to have them slip off an disappear. I will count my own steps and assume that my heart is beating and that I am really sleeping at 4 am. Grrrrrrrrrrrrrr.

title: Made my wrist ache
text: I absolutely loved this watch but it made my wrist ache so I had to return it.  There is something about the sensors they use that after a few minutes of wearing the watch, the ache would start in my wrist and then up into my hand.  I took it off, rested for a period of time and tried it again - with the same result.  I know it's specific to this watch because I currently wear a Samsung Galaxy watch3 and have no discomfort.  The reason I got this watch was because I have a Pixel 3a and the Samsung watch does not fully integrate with my phone, I need a WearOS watch.  Amazon customer service was wonderful and I am able to return the watch for a full refund.  I'm bummed.  I really liked the watch's functionality and integration with my phone.  The only two things I didn't love about the watch:  1) The plastic case - because when it vibrated, it was loud due to the plastic housing.  2) The software didn't have an activity monitor that reminded me to get up and move.  I use this every day with my Samsung watch.  But I would have been very happy to keep the watch if only it didn't make my wrist hurt.

title: 10 faces outta 10 ))))))))))
text:
Firstly, I didn't get this at Amazon, but someone else had a huge sale on them. I was compelled to add this review.

At the price that ?????? sold this watch, 5 stars.
CONS: At the listed retail price, I may have taken a star off, but read to the end. I don't care for the two finger swiping, and endless tapping on things to activate, don't activate, or do or don't deactivate. Menu seems to be really confusing and esoteric, or not intelligent. I have big fingers and did get the bigger 44mm watch, but with two fingers, you cannot see what you are swiping to or from. These are all minor gripings. AND it talks too much, haha.

PROS: Doesn't quite sound like a 5 outta 5 star review so far does it? People are so jaded today, and complain about technology that didn't even exist a few short years ago. Enjoy and marvel at things folks. We always had phones, but today they are computers, and people find a way to nit pic inconsequential things. Look at the big picture. The watch that Samsung has put together is a masterpiece. Sure it isn't perfect, but who wants a Timex or Casio watch now if you own this Samsung Smart Watch? I'm not going to get into all the things it does, because you could write a book on it.

The watch faces that come embedded in the watch are truly great. You can download plenty more free faces too. It will take you a while to find them and refind them. I haven't paid for one yet. I put a couple in my list just to demonstrate the watch to my friends, and has flashing neon colored lights and info galore. Many of the faces have bio signals too. I was really looking for a watch, but I think it is making me healthier, why? Many of the faces tell you to get off your BU** and move around, or what your heart rate is currently beating. I've notice that eating a little healthier, especially less fatting meats, my BPM(beats per minute) have plummeted by ten most all day, and around 60bpm at wake up in the morning. No stressed out wake up heart rate. It is making a believer out of me. There are all kinds of activity and monitor based apps, alarms, phone, music, texts, emails, Facebook, Messenger, and much more. I own a Samsung Note 8 phone and BAM, and meshes with this in a second.

The physical watch itself, is stunning. I got the all black one. Way cool looking. Worth the price all by itself!! The watch face pops up and it is a 'WO THAT LOOKS UNREAL'!! Don't be fooled by the bland yellow stock face they show you, stunners await. The watch band is the best watch band you'll ever put on yer wrist. All the Casio-Timex watches I ever owned, the watch band barely fit my big wrist, and always on the last notch. This strap tucks under the band so it never is flopping out. The notch I use is far from the end, and the band is very high quality soft rubber that is easy to strap on. The strap, I'd rate it higher than the watch itself. There is also a litany of watch straps you can buy too, but the shipped band is incredible. Costco also bundled an extra watch charger. Here's a big tip and is a hint at most folks biggest complaint. So many complainers, so little time. Everyone gets this watch and turns everything on at once. Battery life for that scenario is about a day or less, and then the complainers start in. I learned right away from reading the complainers. I programmed the watch to sleep or go dark in ten seconds, maybe five. Turned the volume down, and shut off most of the monitoring apps.. Most of these things, most of you won't use, so save battery life. The people that use all the apps for jogging and monitoring, just need to realize that charging it is a way of life, or using it for jogging and not every day use. After deprogramming the thing, I get several days out of a charge, and it is still talking at me and doing some monitoring. I laugh when it keeps telling me to get up and move my body while watching sports for too long. Watch can also be set for watch only, which would last for who knows how long on a charge. Don't complain, just buy it, and enjoy the wonders.

I had a Sony Smart watch a few years back and kept breaking the one tiny piece of plastic on it that cost $00.002 cents and ruined the strap or the watch or both. This Samsung edition cost twice as much as that first smart watch and does ten times the features and looks ten times better at $150 on sale. Wait for the sale price and get one, because you might as well enjoy what technology is inventing these days, before we are gone. Don't let the world pass you by.

No stressed out wake up heart rate. It is making a believer out of me. There are all kinds of activity and monitor based apps, alarms, phone, music, texts, emails, Facebook, Messenger, and much more. I own a Samsung Note 8 phone and BAM, and meshes with this in a second.

The physical watch itself, is stunning. I got the all black one. Way cool looking. Worth the price all by itself!! The watch face pops up and it is a 'WO THAT LOOKS UNREAL'!! Don't be fooled by the bland yellow stock face they show you, stunners await. The watch band is the best watch band you'll ever put on yer wrist. All the Casio-Timex watches I ever owned, the watch band barely fit my big wrist, and always on the last notch. This strap tucks under the band so it never is flopping out. The notch I use is far from the end, and the band is very high quality soft rubber that is easy to strap on. The strap, I'd rate it higher than the watch itself. There is also a litany of watch straps you can buy too, but the shipped band is incredible. Costco also bundled an extra watch charger. Here's a big tip and is a hint at most folks biggest complaint. So many complainers, so little time. Everyone gets this watch and turns everything on at once. Battery life for that scenario is about a day or less, and then the complainers start in. I learned right away from reading the complainers. I programmed the watch to sleep or go dark in ten seconds, maybe five. Turned the volume down, and shut off most of the monitoring apps.. Most of these things, most of you won't use, so save battery life. The people that use all the apps for jogging and monitoring, just need to realize that charging it is a way of life, or using it for jogging and not every day use. After deprogramming the thing, I get several days out of a charge, and it is still talking at me and doing some monitoring. I laugh when it keeps telling me to get up and move my body while watching sports for too long. Watch can also be set for watch only, which would last for who knows how long on a charge. Don't complain, just buy it, and enjoy the wonders.

I had a Sony Smart watch a few years back and kept breaking the one tiny piece of plastic on it that cost $00.002 cents and ruined the strap or the watch or both. This Samsung edition cost twice as much as that first smart watch and does ten times the features and looks ten times better at $150 on sale. Wait for the sale price and get one, because you might as well enjoy what technology is inventing these days, before we are gone. Don't let the world pass you by.

title: Exceptional rugged fitness watch
text: So I had several Fit bit bands prior (Charge 2, Charge 3 and Versa) and  Galaxy Active watch. Neither of the Charge bands could be seen in daylight and I had horrific app connectivity issues when trying to sync. The Versa was better. I gifted that to my MIL when I got the Galaxy Active. I liked that interface BUT 2 day battery life was not acceptable. I looked around and found the TRex Pro and dug the rugged look and marketing of long battery life. I easily get 20 days between charges. This to me should be a minimum. The Zepp app has never had an issue on my Samsung Galaxy phone and is detailed. All updates have been seamless and NEVER a sync issue. I like that I get notifications and can choose to respond on my phone or not. I liked mine so much I bought a second one for our son and he absolutely digs his.

title: Awesome Buds for Certain Users.
text:
I think most reviewers that give negative reviews on these completely miss the point with these buds and dont understand the product. The top bullet point on the Samsung product page says "Go Phone Free." The second bullet point is "Simply Tap to Control." I dont know, but it seems to me from the top two bullet points that Samsung primarily intends these to be used without a phone. As it turns out, that is exactly what these are and where they shine.

If you really think about it, not having full, robust, and total control from a phone - Android or iPhone - is irrelevant. Not being able to upload songs directly from an iPhone is irrelevant. The best way to upload songs to these is from a PC, i.e. without a phone. Not being able to have "full" control from an Android or iPhone is irrelevant. The best way to control these is from the touch interface, i.e. without a phone. Not being able to use Bixby on an iPhone is irrelevant. Bixby needs internet and ties you to a phone, which is what you are supposed to be getting away from. If any of your complaints are I wish it would work better with my phone, then your complaint is irrelevant. You are supposed to get away from the phone with these.

Having said all that, the sound quality is above average for ear bud style headphones. The sound is crisp and clean whether via bluetooth or onboard music. The bass is lacking, yes, that is true. But, compared to other similar type headsets, it is at least on par. Overall, the sound is 4/5 compared to other ear buds I have tried.

Bluetooth wireless range is the best I have seen in similar products. I have had cheap wireless earbuds that disconnected 3 feet away. These are full on bluetooth range. If they are cutting out, its the other end of the Bluetooth connection at fault. Its not these earbuds, they are solid. You can transfer songs via Bluetooth, but I believe it has to be one at a time. This is tedious. Again, you are better off using the PC. Connection range and strength 5/5.

Control via hardware is fine. It does take a little practice, but it is better than adequate. Again, the whole point is to get away from the phone. I do wish you could control the songs onboard via a watch. You can stream from a watch and control that just fine. I do not think it is a big ask to be able to control the onboard songs via a watch (certainly from a Samsung watch). Part of the pitch with these is that they are workout ready. I dont want to have my phone when working out (although you can control the onboard songs via the phone). However, I do wear my watch (also pitched for workouts). Only makes sense that the Icon X and (Samsung) watches play better together. I hope they add this feature in the future. Controls 4/5.

Battery life is more than adequate (2018 version) for the intended use. These are not meant to be all day wear. These are meant to be for occasional use. Primarily these are for workouts. When kept in the included charging box (which is highly recommended), these are good for 7 days or more of workouts without having to recharge the box. In this context, no one has a legitimate complaint about battery life. All week battery life for workouts is really way more than anyone can ask. Battery life 5/5.

Comfort is really going to depend on your ear. Everyone has different ears. I found that the ear canal part was fine out of the box. I did change the other piece to the smaller set. I think they fit rather well, again, considering that these are for shorter term use. Comfort 4/5.

The onboard storage is waaaaaay more than needed. Honestly, you dont want more than a couple hundred songs on board at most. I find that 60-75 songs work best. Again, I want this experience to be simple and easy to use without the phone. Too many songs can be a burden to scroll through and find that one song you want to hear right now. Do yourself a favor and dont try to load your entire library on board. Just keep your favorites on the buds. It is better to have bulk storage on a watch (or phone if you want to have that on you for workouts), as it is much easier to find and play songs in a large catalog on these. Storage 5/5.

Final thoughts. If you understand this product and how to best use it, it is hard to deny that Samsung has hit a homerun with these. They have it where it counts. If you are an audiophile, these are not for you. If you want to have and control huge libraries of music, these are not for you. If you want all day every day wear, these are not for you. If you insist on doing everything from a phone, these are not for you. But within a certain space for a certain user, these are the best on the market. Yes, they are pricey. I feel that these should be closer to $100 mainly because these definitely purpose, and not something you will want to use all the time in every situation. Overall 4.5/5.
