title: A million times better than Fitbit
text: I initially bought a fitbit for my son, but returned it after a day because it was so awful. I looked online for an alternative and found the Galaxy Fit 2. I have had other Samsung watches, but I didnt know that they had recently come out with the Fit 2. It was less than half the price of the fitbit and so so much better. The only thing my son would like is to be able to change the watch band. As it is still new we couldn't find any - but we'll keep looking. I'm waiting for it to go on sale again so I can buy one for myself. Very pleased!

title: Expensive, Good, & Takes Some Getting Use To
text:
When did finding earphones to play music get so complicated? Remember the good old days when Sony earphones were just ten bucks? It was wired, fit in one’s ears, and did the trick. Now, it is just endless options and many of them are overpriced and aren’t even that great. I’ll keep my review short.

I have the Samsung S9 Plus & the included wired AKG tuned (Samsung branded) earbuds are fine, and I actually like it, but the wire has cotton fabric on the outside to make it less susceptible to getting tangled. However, when it does get tangled, it is actually very annoying to get untangled. Plus, it clings to clothes way more than usual earphones made of plastic/rubber.

I got these and I am happy with it overall, but it does take some getting used to. They fit great in the ear, appear well made, and look futuristic and sexy (unlike the Apple ones that are white, and look unattractive, like something flaccid). The sound is fine, but i do wish it was slightly a smidge louder with maybe a little extra bass. Please keep in mind the earbuds automatically start consuming battery once it is out of the charging case, so one has to be really careful on they how store these before actually putting them to use.

Pros:
Great design
Decent battery Life
Case doubles up as protection and charges the earbuds without a cord (functions as extra portable battery pack)
UBS C type charging port for quick charge
Functions has a solo MP3 player without a phone
Can be used for talking and making phone calls
Samsung Gear software already preinstalled on Samsung phones
Ten mins of charging gets a full hour of music play

Cons:
Takes some getting use to
Super small and paranoid that it can get lost at any min without delicate care
Battery starts working as soon as they are taken out of the case
No On/Off button
No USB C charging brick included (For this price tag it should have come with one)
No extra case (For this price tag it should have come with one)
Sound could be slightly louder with extra bass (Just a small smidge)
Expensive and overpriced
Amazon included this with 40lbs of kitty litter and item came smashed

title: Battery life is not great, good watch though
text:
Update... I am not sure what changed, possibly a software update did it. But I started getting much better battery life out of this watch. I just got in the habit of charging it every night and using super conservative power settings for pretty much everything, and before it was a struggle to make it all day in a charge. And then all the sudden it wasn't an issue anymore. Now when I put it on the charger at night if sometimes still has 80% battery left.

So a typical Samsung mobile device. Good hardware, bad software. But I updated my review to not be so critical because I don't have anything to complain about at the moment.

My original review below.

I bought this to replace an older Galaxy Watch that lasted me a few years. I was initially very happy with this watch.

But then after about 3 months, the battery life started going way downhill. When it was new, it could go 2-3 days before it needed to charge. Now, it can't even make it 12 hours before complaining about low battery - and that's with every possible power saving feature on. GPS is off. All the features that turn on the screen are disabled - the only way the screen turns on is by me pressing the power/home button. And even with the "battery saver" mode enabled, now today it is down to 5% battery after wearing it for 14 hours and barely even turning the screen on.

This model is crap when it comes to battery life. Stay away!

But then after about 3 months, the battery life started going way downhill. When it was new, it could go 2-3 days before it needed to charge. Now, it can't even make it 12 hours before complaining about low battery - and that's with every possible power saving feature on. GPS is off. All the features that turn on the screen are disabled - the only way the screen turns on is by me pressing the power/home button. And even with the "battery saver" mode enabled, now today it is down to 5% battery after wearing it for 14 hours and barely even turning the screen on.

This model is crap when it comes to battery life. Stay away!

title: Not a bad watch for my 1st smart watch
text:
I am writing this review having never had a smart watch before (except 1 VERY BAD and CHEAP smartwatch, that lasted exactly 3 hours).

This Samsung Watch 4 40mm watch is almost everything I wanted a smart watch to be.
Setup took a LOT longer than I thought it would.
I had to update the watch through my phone using bluetooth, which was horribly slow.
I also had to update my phone to be able to access the watch updating properly (I think it was 3 apps for the phone to be able to update the watch, which took an hour), then I had to download and install a few more apps for my phone to be able to mod the watch and locate it if stolen and get watch faces.
Then there was the setup for each and every app that the watch needed, with tutorials (which I needed, since this is the first REAL smart watch I have owned).
All through bluetooth.
I think it took me 3 to 4 hours to get the watch, and my phone, totally updated to use (on my phone, I uninstalled anything I didnt need, or use, or want, long before getting this watch, so I had a LOT of apps to download and install from samsung just for my phone. If you have left your samsung phone with all the apps still on it and updated it all the time, then it will take you much less time than it took me).

I like EVERYTHING about this Watch 4, except 2 things:

1. Bixby. Piece of junk software that I dont need, dont want, and there is NO WAY to turn it off or get rid of the icon, or even disable it. I accidentally hit it 5 to 6 times in 2 days. Samsung, PLEASE at LEAST let us users be able to disable Bixby.

2. The band, or strap, or whatever its called. It would be great, IF it didnt make you put one side of the band UNDER the other side to lock it in. This pulls my arm hair, pinches my skin, and creates a bump under my wrist. Terrible, terrible design for a watch band. Its too bad too, because other than that, it is a comfortable watch band.

Other than those 2 issues, the watch is great.
After using it for a few days, and having Battery Saver on (which does 2 things. It makes the battery last for days. And, it also unfortunately disables a couple things I actually use, so I have to turn it off to use things I want, then turn it back on to save battery, which isnt as easy as it is on a phone).

The charge time is really, really fast (even during the 4 hour setup, which made the watch go down to 21%, it charged fully in just under 1/2 an hour).

I am disabled and have a lot of health issues, and I can use ECG and all the other health apps including sleep, during the day and night, and save the results to my phone which makes a chart for a month of all the results, which is a super feature, as I dont need multiple devices anymore, nor do I need to write results down anymore. To be honest, the ecg, bpm, pulseox, etc, is the main reason I bought the watch. And, the results are pretty close to what the Dr.s offices record, so its pretty accurate.

God forgive me, but I like being able to switch watch faces also. I wish samsung had a LOT more free watch faces, and I also wish they made it so that Ads dont play, but I love being able to go from stainless, to gold, to black, from analog to digital, to being able to actually customize where everything is displayed, and what I want to remove from being displayed. It is so much easier to look at my watch than to log into my phone to see the weather, or calander, or whatever.

Oh, and another thing I dont really care for, but is my fault, is the way to scroll. Never having had a smart watch before, let alone a samsung smart watch, I did not know that you scroll with the side of the screen. I keep launching apps I didnt mean to because of the scroll feature. I should have gotten the Watch 4 Classic, but I didnt know the wheel actually scrolled through everything until after I got the regular Watch 4. So, thats my bad, not samsungs fault.

I would definitely reccomend the Watch 4, especially for those with health issues. This is the best watch I have ever bought in my life. If you are an android samsung user, definitely pick one up.

title: Not a smart watch, definitely a tracker, but exactly what I needed.
text:
Let me first say that I am not a smart watch person. I had the Samsung Watch before and absolutely hated it. I was very resistant to switch from my faithful Charge 3 to a new device that seemed similar to a smart watch, especially one that clearly had features that hadn't been fully sorted out yet. At the same time, some of these features, like the 24/7 heartrate monitoring and improved data for sleep monitoring were definitely a draw.

I haven't had this device for months yet, and may update this in the future if I notice different things, but I will say if you go into this with the idea that you're buying a health tracker, you'll get about what you expect. This is not a fancy smart watch and if you want all the music features, additional apps, and things of that nature, this device is not for you. It doesn't function like a smart watch near at all. The music player is limited to Deezer, which I haven't tried. It allows you to navigate Pandora and Spotify on your phone through the device, which is literally the only feature I wanted a smart watch for when I was teaching dance, so for me it's perfect. Almost everything else focuses on your health.

Other reviews have commented on the press point to activate the watch being problematic, but I have had no issues. It doesn't always come on when I turn my wrist to face, which was the same issue with all my previous Fitbit devices. However, the press point to turn it on is basically the same idea as my Charge 3, so maybe that's why I have no issues there.

Not all the newly available metrics have obvious uses. The additional sleep data is very much up for interpretation, but if you keep a log with how you feel when you wake in the morning, any correlation becomes obvious. While I don't know how to interpret this data myself, it is something I've mentioned to my doctor and we will be reviewing on my next visit to help give us a bigger picture of what contributes to my poor quality sleep and other sleep challenges. This is one of the reasons I opted to upgrade when my Charge 3 was still working great and perfectly serviceable.

In additional metrics, we should talk about that EDA scan that's mentioned in all the reviews, but no one seems to understand. I'm actively trying to train myself to make use of that feature. A nifty little thing I've noticed, EDA responses don't appear when I'm under high stress, but they do appear when I've experimented with using the quick scan feature as a non-visual timer for deep breathing in the middle of a anxiety attack. While I don't know if this is reliable, it is giving my medical team more information to try and figure out what's going on, so this may have useful implication in the future.

Another note on the EDA quick scan, as I haven't done any of the guided sessions, it gives you information on your heartrate variability in comparison to your baseline, which I assume is taken from your sleep baseline. Below baseline indicates high stress where above indicates a relaxed state. For me, this has become a useful tool to make sure I'm getting enough exercise, which directly impacts my depression. I know some people out there say "just make sure you get XYZ amount of exercise every day." For me this is giving me useful data on how much and how often is enough to get that number up. Will a run or yoga in the morning be effective for the day? Does an evening routine impact the next morning? It's quantifiable data that I can use as a cue in building my own routine.

I also want to mention the other feature that sold me on this device, the 24/7 heartrate monitoring. Before the pandemic (and became increasingly difficult throughout the pandemic) I had taken up running. One of the things my training app asked was my heartrate during my run. Only problem was I could not accurately get that data as my Charge 3 needed me to remain stationary for an accurate heartrate read. While my heartrate on a run may not have been a necessary detail to include, not having that heartrate often meant exercises were missed and other activities that possibly should have flagged a response didn't. My active minutes have dramatically increased as a result of having more accurate heartrate monitoring. I am also getting a much more accurate look at my activity levels and my calorie intake versus calories burned. This constant data is a lot more useful for anyone who is monitoring their health during exercise, trying to get an accurate calories in/calories out record, and anyone trying to get an accurate picture of what their activity level is throughout the day beyond simply steps to include things previous trackers may have missed. It even picked up on 4 minutes of cardio levels while I was doing an excited sword demonstration for my kids while homeschooling. Is that 4 minutes useful time? Probably not, but it's good to put in my list of activities Fitbit wouldn't have noticed before, but logs now, which also includes dance and other movement exercises that weren't enough to trigger my Charge 3 to identify them as exercise. It was disappointing that 2 hours of dance 3 days a week would often only show up as maybe 30 minutes each day, and sometimes not at all. Those same practices (with an admittedly shorter 30 minute cap due to lack or stamina, thanks pandemic...) are being picked up in their entirety, even if my heartrate elevation is small, but enough to push it into registering in one of the "zones". This has helped a good deal in getting an overall portrait of my health and activity levels, which I'll be bringing to my doctor on my next visit.

The only thing I have found is I haven't been able to get 6 days of battery life out of it, generally landing at 4-5 days. For me this isn't an issue as I don't wear my device to shower and have had a habit since my original Charge HR (way back in the day) of popping it on the charger whenever I shower. This may also be why I don't have any effect from the device or band as my wrist gets regular breaks.

If you're primarily looking for a smart watch and just want something with some extra features, this will not be the device for you. While the extra health tracking is a great draw, it loses a lot of the functionality my old smart watch had, the notifications for things like texts don't always come through (which is fine for me because I don't want that) and the pay feature isn't fantastic. Again, that doesn't matter to me because it's a feature I don't intend to really use anyway, but had to test it out. The apps are limited, and options for music and taking calls aren't wonderful.

On the other hand, if you're looking for a device to give you detailed health information, to accurately log fitness activities, and increased metrics for sleep, this may just be the device for you. It's the best fitness tracker I've seen so far, and this is giving my medical team some new information to work with, which may or may not be useful in the long run, but looks promising. That EDA quick scan had potential for people who suffer clinical anxiety or even PTSD as it can be used to cue deep breathing, and the resulting data may result in creating a better profile of what's going on during an attack, or at the very least can result in creating a cue to take specific actions in a way that simply feeling in the moment cannot. Not all of this data will be useful for everyone, but it could be useful for someone who needs this kind of data to help understand their sleep patterns, anxiety levels, how activity, even in small amounts of a few minutes here and there, impacts their mental health, or even just someone who wants an accurate profile of their physical fitness throughout their exercises.

I was 100% resistant to the idea of yet another smart watch, but in the end I'm happy I made the switch. The added information I've gained has given me a lot more understanding of what's going on with me. I now have clues as to why nights I get plenty of quality sleep (according to previously going on just my sleep score) I wake up feeling tired, and I'm seeing a correlation with sleep temp particularly and low quality sleep. That 24/7 heartrate has been huge. I can now actually monitor my activity level instead of trying to track exercise my Charge missed (because I never turn on exercises prior to start as I never remember or can't figure out which fits best), and result in trying to figure out a routine that way. Everything is logged now, making it simple and accurate. The EDA scan is becoming a tool to manage my anxiety, and while the metrics may or may not be useful, it does cue activities which are useful, so even if it's no more than placebo effect, the end result is valuable.

What you will get out of this device will be very personal and it depends on how you use it, and what you expect to get from it. The Fitbit Sense can be a useful tool for you and your medical team, or even you and your trainer, depending on your goals. This device is definitely not for everyone, and the price point does not make it worth it for every user, but if you're looking for a tool to track your health, this one seems to be the best on the market to date.

high stress where above indicates a relaxed state for me this has become a useful tool to make sure i m getting enough exercise which directly impacts my depression i know some people out there say just make sure you get xyz amount of exercise every day for me this is giving me useful data on how much and how often is enough to get that number up will a run or yoga in the morning be effective for the day does an evening routine impact the next morning it s quantifiable data that i can use as a cue in building my own routine i also want to mention the other feature that sold me on this device the heartrate monitoring before the pandemic and became increasingly difficult throughout the pandemic i had taken up running one of the things my training app asked was my heartrate during my run only problem was i could not accurately get that data as my charge needed me to remain stationary for an accurate heartrate read while my heartrate on a run may not have been a necessary detail to include not having that heartrate often meant exercises were missed and other activities that possibly should have flagged a response didn t my active minutes have dramatically increased as a result of having more accurate heartrate monitoring i am also getting a much more accurate look at my activity levels and my calorie intake versus calories burned this constant data is a lot more useful for anyone who is monitoring their health during exercise trying to get an accurate calories in calories out record and anyone trying to get an accurate picture of what their activity level is throughout the day beyond simply steps to include things previous trackers may have missed it even picked up on minutes of cardio levels while i was doing an excited sword demonstration for my kids while homeschooling is that minutes useful time probably not but it s good to put in my list of activities fitbit wouldn t have noticed before but logs now which also includes dance and other movement exercises that weren t enough to trigger my charge to identify them as exercise it was disappointing that hours of dance days a week would often only show up as maybe minutes each day and sometimes not at all those same practices with an admittedly shorter minute cap due to lack or stamina thanks pandemic are being picked up in their entirety even if my heartrate elevation is small but enough to push it into registering in one of the zones this has helped a good deal in getting an overall portrait of my health and activity levels which i ll be bringing to my doctor on my next visit the only thing i have found is i haven t been able to get days of battery life out of it generally landing at days for me this isn t an issue as i don t wear my device to shower and have had a habit since my original charge hr way back in the day of popping it on the charger whenever i shower this may also be why i don t have any effect from the device or band as my wrist gets regular breaks if you re primarily looking for a smart watch and just want something with some extra features this will not be the device for you while the extra health tracking is a great draw it loses a lot of the functionality my old smart watch had the notifications for things like texts don t always come through which is fine for me because i don t want that and the pay feature isn t fantastic again that doesn t matter to me because it s a feature i don t intend to really use anyway but had to test it out the apps are limited and options for music and taking calls aren t wonderful on the other hand if you re looking for a device to give you detailed health information to accurately log fitness activities and increased metrics for sleep this may just be the device for you it s the best fitness tracker i ve seen so far and this is giving my medical team some new information to work with which may or may not be useful in the long run but looks promising that eda quick scan had potential for people who suffer clinical anxiety or even ptsd as it can be used to cue deep breathing and the resulting data may result in creating a better profile of what s going on during an attack or at the very least can result in creating a cue to take specific actions in a way that simply feeling in the moment cannot not all of this data will be useful for everyone but it could be useful for someone who needs this kind of data to help understand their sleep patterns anxiety levels how activity even in small amounts of a few minutes here and there impacts their mental health or even just someone who wants an accurate profile of their physical fitness throughout their exercises i was resistant to the idea of yet another smart watch but in the end i m happy i made the switch the added information i ve gained has given me a lot more understanding of what s going on with me i now have clues as to why nights i get plenty of quality sleep according to previously going on just my sleep score i wake up feeling tired and i m seeing a correlation with sleep temp particularly and low quality sleep that heartrate has been huge i can now actually monitor my activity level instead of trying to track exercise my charge missed because i never turn on exercises prior to start as i never remember or can t figure out which fits best and result in trying to figure out a routine that way everything is logged now making it simple and accurate the eda scan is becoming a tool to manage my anxiety and while the metrics may or may not be useful it does cue activities which are useful so even if it s no more than placebo effect the end result is valuable what you will get out of this device will be very personal and it depends on how you use it and what you expect to get from it the fitbit sense can be a useful tool for you and your medical team or even you and your trainer depending on your goals this device is definitely not for everyone and the price point does not make it worth it for every user but if you re looking for a tool to track your health this one seems to be the best on the market to date

title: Insane Battery Life
text: I owned Samsung watches before, but as soon as the warranty ran out, they stopped working. It really stings to lose hundreds of dollars. This watch, on the other hand (pardon the pun,) is amazing. It does everything they say it will and then some. I've only had it for about 2 weeks, but everything is working great so far. Oh and yes, I charged it when I first got it, and it's still going on the same charge. Crazy! The app that manages the watch is adequate, and it doesn't seem to drain my cell phone. Always a plus. I haven't tried all the sports settings, and I probably never will, there are so many, but being a gym rat and an avid outdoors person, I couldn't have asked for anything more. I would recommend it to anyone that is tired of paying 3 times as much for a watch that does about half of what this one does.

title: Good Budget Smartwatch - Setup/App Not So Great
text:
Let's start off with a quick statement about this watch. I purchased this on sale for right around $35, so my expectations were pretty low to start with. If you are looking for high quality and a lot of features, this is not a watch for you. Also I would like to mention that I am using an Android Phone (Samsung S22). With that in mind, here is my review, Pros/Cons first then a brief summary:

Pros:
- Price: If you can pick this up on sale, this is a good deal. I was able to get mine for $35, which was about 50% of the price. I feel like I could work in this and not worry about breaking it like I would a higher priced watch.
- Batter Life: The battery seems to last quite a while between charges. I'm going on about 4 days now without charging.
Comfort: The smaller face on this watch makes it a bit more confortable than others I've used. The band is soft and curves well around my arm.
- Looks: This does not look bad for a cheap watch. You can tell that the band is not high quality, but as far as looks, this is not a bad looking watch.

Cons:
- Functionality: This has very few features. If you are wanting something that you can type on, talk through, play games on, ect, then this will sorely disappoint. It has the basic health type features (heart rate, O2, sleep monitor, steps...stuff like that). It has the ability to receive text and weather, but you really can't do anything with it other than view.
- App/Setup: This is the biggest gripe from me. It was a pain to set up compared to some of the more expensive ones. First, the app is very basic, with little to no help getting things working. My weather did not work initially, it would crash the app. It started off in C when I wanted it in F. I thought I could change it in the watch...no you change it in the app by clicking the temp. I finally got that working only to find my messaging wouldn't work. After messing around with all of the various permissions, it started to work. Definitely not something that I had to do with my Samsung watch.
- Movement detection: There are times when I go to move my arm and the watch face acts as though I want to change it (it gets small and vibrates). Not sure what that is...probably something I could turn off in the app.

When I first started to set this up, I was pretty disappointed in it. The weather did not work, the watch settings were very basic, the settings were set up in a way that I really did not like, and the app was not very friendly. The setup is not user friendly at all, but once things are set up, the watch works great! All of my features are working now, so I shouldn't have to really get back into the app unless I want to change my watch face.
The features of this watch are limited, unlike watches like the Samsung and Apple brand, but you also pay for those features. Me, I like simple. I have a phone that I can use for things like games, texting, and calls. This has just enough for me to monitor when I need to. If that is not what you're after, pass this one up.
I would recommend this if you can pick it up for under $40. It is my opinion that this is a good budget watch if you want something you are not afraid to break or replace. I'm giving it 4 stars, with the note that this is taking out the trouble with the app and only focused on the watch functionality. I've rated this in the app store already.

title: pimptastic headphones (not so much)
text:
Updated review after a couple weeks use:

It is ok. But lately it's been dropping signal even when I'm holding my galaxy note 4, so its not because of distance. And the note 4 has the latency software you say works with these headphones. Btw I've never had Bluetooth latency, not even on a cheap 20$ pair of Bluetooth headphones.

It has really bad switching between my iPad air and my note, to the point that its not useful. Check out the Samsung gear headphones to see a really well done multi machine connection setup, its seamless, so I know it can be done right and cheaply.

It barely works on my iPad, every time I start a new show it drops audio but not the connection so I have to turn them off and on, or reconnect in settings. This is unacceptable.

It's got great battery life, and its got decent sound, but it's not pro anything. Its Bluetooth and so I give these a lot more room for failure because Bluetooth cans are not being made in as much quantity as jaybird knockoffs, but maybe that'll change.

Worth 100 bucks? Maybe. It does what you say it does, just barely and not very well.

For example my 30$ samsung gear headphones are better quality wise in every way, but they're not cans. They connect to all devices equally and switch fast and seamlessly.

I doubt seriously your pro edition is any better than your 50 dollar option, my guess is its exactly the same quality and same issues.

So while not being horrible, they're not exactly awesome. And I feel cheated for getting the more expensive option when they're most likely the exact same as the cheaper edition, I mean if they were worse you couldn't actually use them.

Old review right after I got them:
I just got these and they came fully charged, I have been jammin' out all day!
connected to all my devices, and it does switching really well, which is one of the main reasons i got this.
I don't know about the low latency stuff, but my phone has the apx stuff and while I have never noticed lag in audio on Anything I've ever owned that does audio, that could just be because I buy quality when it comes to my ears ;).

Update: So apparently you have to use the provided usb cable to charge these. i don't know what is different about them, but the charge light would shut off after a couple seconds, which kinda sucks.
but on the other hand, I've been listening to these for 3 days and they've only been charged for like 10 minutes. So now I'm going to have a full charge and we'll see how long these last on a full charge.
If the seller sees this:
what kind of usb cable is this? usb3?

title: Not an android watch, for better and worse.
text: I've had a few samsung watches, they're nice but the battery life is short and they break too easily. This watch has less features and doesn't sync with any of the Samsung health stuff. The operating system is simple and there are only a handful of faces available. Still, it keeps time, displays notifications, allows call rejection, counts steps, keeps track of heart rate,and the battery lasts for days. It's a steal for the money. It seems like it's a fairly high quality build. It does charge on my Samsung wireless watch charger as well as it's own charger. I don't even know how long the battery lasts, because I've never let it run longer than 3 days on a charge. In this sense, it's way better than every Samsung watch that I've owned which all were lucky to make it through 1 day.

title: Gear Fit
text: Had my Samsung watch for three weeks and all of a sudden it started to reboot and wouldn't stop for a day.  I requested a replacement from Amazon and they sent the replacement immediately.  Set-up the pick-up for the old one.  Hopefully my new one continues to work longer than three weeks.  I also read on-line that lots of other people are having the same problem.  Hopefully Samsung is working on a fix so it doesn't continue to happen.

title: Image quality at par with others, functionality is subpar
text:
UPDATE:
I used the KeyMission and the Samsung Gear 360 in an Emergency Room scenario video yesterday. Did not use the app to control either device. I just used the apps to change settings and then to eye-ball perspective once they devices were mounted. After that, I manually fired the cameras each time.

Footage: KeyMission footage was more pleasing -- better color rendition, better tones. The Samsung footage was flat in comparison -- usable, but noticeably flat. Exposure was similar between the two. Sharpness is similar between the two. Stitching for objects closer to the camera is a problem for Nikon, but wasn't detrimental to the project. For other uses where objects along the stitching line that are less than 3 feet away are important will be a problem.

Functionality: The KeyMission worked fine. No hiccups. I could tune in via the app between takes if there was time -- and it did take 30 seconds to log into the app and connect each time. The Samsung took maybe 20 seconds to connect each time.

Battery: The KeyMission lasted an hour. Sporadic use of WiFi. Maybe 35 mins of actual footage. The Samsung lasted 48 minutes before it overheated and I swapped in a cooler battery to keep going. So, while I can't compare battery life, the overheating issue for Samsung is almost the same kind of problem.

Post-Process: The Nikon files came out of the camera already stitched and read to be edited. The Samsung files needed to pass through the Samsung phone to be stitched. That took hours. I was able to take the files off the Nikon card and cut a quick one minute sample file for others to see within 30 minutes of the shoot. The only thing that would have made this better is if I could have done it all on a mobile device rather than having to lug a laptop onsite.

So, I've upgraded my review to four stars based on the above experience and plan on keeping my Nikon and using it in place of the Samsung.

ORIGINAL REVIEW BELOW
Bought the 360 at BB yesterday. First thought: pairing to an iPhone 7 and a 6 were equally challenging. I burned through a fully charged battery trying to get it right. It was a matter of doing the steps over and over until it worked; making sure after each failed attempt to make the iPhone forget the Bluetooth and wifi. But in the end, I have it paired to both phones.

Second, once paired and in use, I learned that no matter what you intend to do, you need to turn camera on and then in the app, select Remote Photography in order to make the camera start its wifi network. Even if you are just wanting to review images on the card via the app or change settings, you have to go into Remote Photography first. No network is initiated if you go into the other modes first. Takes 15-20 seconds. And be ready for the connection to drop if you are inactive even for a couple seconds. Or if you go into another app. The Samsung Gear 360 connects no matter what the task and holds for longer.

Third, forget trying to download video to your smartphone. It just errors out, no matter the file size. The Samsung Gear 360 gets it done no problem; too bad it only works with a Samsung phone.

Photos straight from the key mission are not recognized as 360 by Facebook, whether you upload straight from SnapBridge, from iPhone's photo app, or via the Facebook app. Photos from Samsung Gear 360 are recognized by Facebook as 360.

SnapBridge will only upload the 2MP file to Facebook from within the app; it errors out when you try to upload the original.

The desktop software won't run on my Windows 10 machine. Refuses to install. But that's fine since the files straight off the micro-SD card are already stitched and ready for YouTube. Haven't tried other VR video outlets yet. But this is one advantage the key mission 360 has over the Samsung Gear 360. All files from the Samsung camera have to pass through a stitching process if you take the files straight from the card. And since I edit in Adobe Premiere and inject the pano metadata manually anyway, I don't really care to use Nikon's software.

Photo image quality so far is the same between Samsung and Nikon. Video image quality is also close but right now I'd give the low light edge to Nikon (images are soft but not as noisy as Samsung) and the stitching quality for near-distance items are goes Samsung. Daylight or well-lit indoor shots, no straight advantage by either. But the fact there's exposure control on Nikon I think would be great for difficultly-lit situations. However, it's buried deep in the menus and there's no ISO control, which Samsung has.

So, after one day, can I say Nikon's $500 device is worth the money versus Samsung's $350 + a Samsung phone (if you don't own one)? Well, if you own a Samsung, no it's not. If you plan to have a desktop workflow, yes it is. But if you are hoping for a mobile device workflow, I need to do more testing.

Samsung has its functionality problems too and I'll see how Nikon holds up. For example, the Samsung Gear 360 overheats and shuts down after 9 minutes of shooting at the highest video resolution. It also forms condensation inside the lens of the dew point is right. And the round ball shape makes accidental photos and videos possible because you can't rally grab anywhere safely.

title: Bad Experience with Bluetooth Only Watch
text:
First, I bought the Bluetooth edition of this watch and tried everything to get it to work with my phone but it would not connect. I have a brand new Samsung S21 and the Samsung Galaxy Watch 4 40 mm Bluetooth would not recognize the phone or vice versa.

Articles abound on the internet to fix this issue - wipe Bluetooth settings, reset or factory reset watch, try older versions of the Galaxy Wearables app. None of these worked, except factory resetting my phone, which worked for about 6 days before failing again. The watch stopped staying connected and stopped detecting sleep and health indicators. The battery also died extremely quickly.

I took a chance and bought the 44 mm LTE edition of the watch because I loved the feature set of the previous one so much. It paired immediately with my phone, and has stayed connected since. The LTE is making the difference. It costs $5 more on my plan to add the watch, but it's worth it. I have more features enabled on this 44 mm edition and it stays charged through the day without the need for recharge.

I also bought a Fitbit Charge prior to both of these. It would not reliably detect sleep or other health indicators. Fitbit recommended wearing it on the inside of the wrist or not telling it you're going to sleep so it will auto detect what you're doing. This only worked some of the time.

The Galaxy Watch gives the same indicators as the Fitbit and the sensor is more accurate.

title: I really love the watch, but the battery life is horrible.
text:
I switched from a Fitbit to the Samsung Watch after my Fitbit simply kept having connectivity issues. I really liked it and had a few of them over the years. But I will say the Samsung Watch is much nicer overall, except for the battery life. I knew the battery life was a known issue prior to buying it however, but I always put my watches on the charger when showering. So it hasn't been a huge problem, but I only get maybe around 25-26hrs in total before it shuts off. As far as the functionality, it does pretty much as you would expect from a mainstream smart watch. Tons of apps, tracking, push notifications, watch faces, etc. You can also easily make custom faces using any photos, and adjust the type of data the face displays. The dial also spins and allows you to scroll through varies menu's. Tapping, swiping and the side buttons also give some extra ways to interact with it. And as mentioned it supports push notifications, so like most watches all you have to do it flip your wrist up to see message, answer phone calls etc. It even has full texting capability with it's tiny keyboard. Although... that's not something you are probably going to use a lot. Charging wise it charges pretty quickly, so that at least helps balance out the battery issue.

I have been pretty happy with it overall.

title: Wow!
text: I have a Samsung Gear S2 which I love but the screen is too small and the battery life is too short. Because of that, I decided that I was going to order a watch with a bigger screen and longer battery life but didn't want to spend $300.  I did a lot of research and in the end bought this watch because of the positive reviews and I am glad I did. Now don't get me wrong, this watch does not have nearly the same amount of features as the Samsung but it is exactly what I need. All I wanted was a watch with long battery life that could also shows all my text messages, phone calls, emails and notifications. This watch does that and more, it also shows amount of steps, hours of sleep, heart rate etc. And on top of that it is waterproof and the battery life for me has lasted longer than a month. Also, the screen is large, clear and the watch is very nice looking with a nice band. It is somewhat limited on watch faces but they have added new watch faces every week and there are probably over 50 options. Considering the price, the features and it's great appearance, this watch is a no brainier and I would recommend it to anyone!

title: A great Wear OS smartwatch
text:
Update 2019-11: Suddenly the watch isn't charged well, the charging speed was very slow. After googled on the Web, I found the charger issue at the Mobvoi's community forum. Fortunately, my 1-year warranty has still alive, so I created a support ticket. After a few days, they responded to me and sent a brand-new charger. It works very well, I haven't measured but it seems the charging speed increased.

----

It's my first smartwatch, so I can't compare with other smartwatches, but I'm satisfied. After purchased I concerned the weight, but it's lighter than my expectation. It has beautiful display and nice battery life. It has 2 days life in normal use.

There are official replace bands but we can use other bands for Samsung Gear S3. The bundled band looks too formal to me, so I replaced with this band https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B073RFQXPB/

title: Love the Watch Hate the Battery
text:
I Love my Samsung watch but the battery life isn't the best.
The apps work well. You can customize which apps you want and get rid of those you don't. You can always add them later if you change your mind. I don't have to have my phone on me all the time which is great. I can answer calls, which I have done. I can see text messages come in real time. It's a great smartwatch. I just have issues with the battery life. One of my friends limits the apps he uses on his Galaxy watch and gets 2 sometimes 3 days on a single charge. I've done that but prefer to have all the apps I need at the ready, PLUS have a fantastic battery life on my smartwatch.
Fix this issue Samsung! I won't ever use anything Apple. Just fix this please.

title: A nice watch with an annoying flaw
text: In general the Galaxy Watch 3 is a nice watch.  There are two problems with my watch: 1) the floor/stair function is unreliable and does not work most of the time, 2) occasionally the sleep function does not automatically track the sleep period and sleep time must be entered manually.  The biggest negative with this watch is the poor implementation of the floor/step function.  Samsung has  had problems with this feature for years.  They can't get it right while other watch vendors do.  When I see long term unresolved problems  persist while the competition succeeds, I question how much Samsung cares about their product and the level of resource  they are willing to commit to fixing nagging problems.

title: Best Yet!
text:
I want to start off with what I didn't like with this device because its not a lot. Firstly is the bezel, its not the worst thing in the world but it is very noticeable and is worse than some of the competition or even previous ticwatches. The other issue is with using Google Assistant, on the phone usually when you ask google it will show a confirmation that it did what was asked but on the watch it will just close out and you are left wondering if the right thing was done or if anything was done. Usually everything has been correct but i wish they displayed like a checkmark or something to confirm what was done.

Moving on to the good stuff is everything else, #1 is the speed, this is by far the most responsive watch I've owned and finally something that I feel is comparable to apple watch in performance without the price of something the like galaxy gear devices. The screen is sharp and clear, and everything works as expected. At $200 it is hard to imagine anything that is this powerful. I've owned the fossil smartwatch, samsung smartwatch, and several Ticwatches, this is the second watch after the galaxy that I felt was actually helpful and not a hinderance and at its price I feel like it is worth as opposed to the galaxy watch which feels a little too much for what a smartwatch is used for. Battery life is incredible, the new processor in this device really works magic.

title: Very good value. Phone calls not great, no ambient sound options which is a bummer.
text:
Update update.
I got in touch with anker direct customer service. They offer an 18 month warranty on their products from defects. They were awesome to work with, and they communicated very well. They paid for shipping for me to send my broken earbuds back to them, and once they received them, they were very quick to send me a brand new pair no hassle. Their customer service is great.

Update. They quit working one day after the return window closed. The left earbud only flashes a red light.

I have tried a few different ear buds, and these by far are the best bang for the buck. They are my wife's, but I used them for a few days and they are great.

My first pair of ear buds were the samsung gear icon x 2018. They killed my ears, and the touch pads on the side were horrible. The zolo liberty ear buds are very comfortable for me for 2 to 3 hours of use. After that I experience ear fatigue. I have yet to find a set of ear buds that don't fatigue my ears. I think it's just part of the deal. These, however, for my ears are some of the most comfortable I have tried. Fitment is subjective. Everyone's ears are different.

As far as battery life goes, these are great. I get 3 hour of listening out of them before they need a charge. The case will charge the buds 3 or 4 times before it's dead. Also, you can store them in the case for quite a while. It took me over a week to drain them to the point that the case needed a charge.

As far as sound goes, these are great. They have very responsive and immerseful lows mids and highs. Compared to the Samsung icon x's, these sound better, in my opinion.

These do make Bluetooth phone calls. The listeners on the other end said the quality was ok. I didn't have any problem communicating or hearing anyone. However, these ear buds seal off your ear canal, so your own voice is heard through your bones, which makes talking weird. It's like talking with earplugs in. The sound only comes from the right earbud which is also weird. The Samsung's, as well as the jabra elite 65t have ambient sound turned on while using the phone, and the sound comes from both ear buds, which is much better. Calling on the zolo's works, but there are better options available for calls.

Overall, these are pretty comfortable, quality is great, battery life is great, value is great. Phone calls are not the strong point of these.

title: Not bad for price. Too big for wrist!
text:
So right off the bat, it is too big. I think the wristband is adjustable but I have yet to figure out how to do it without damaging it. As it is now it is too big for me to wear. (My wrists are average size for an adult woman.)

Secondly, there are a handful of typos in the instruction book. Missing letters, misspelled words, etc. The instruction book contains 5-6 languages though. Also a little round plastic disc shape fell out of it when I opened it, with a “front” and “back” stickers on each face. Not sure what the purpose of this thing is, it is just slightly smaller than the watch face but it’s not adhesive, so no idea.

Anyway, I’m still messing with it. It is very pretty And even if it’s only usable as a watch and step counter I’d be happy with it. It’s not a bad price and I don’t need all the bells and whistles of say, an Apple or Samsung smart watch. For the price, it’s not bad. I just hope I can figure out how to adjust the strap so I can wear it.

title: Battery life stinks
text:
So I upgraded from my fitbit versa 2 to the galaxy watch 4. I knew the battery life on the galaxy watch would be a huge downgrade from the fit bit which I only have to charge once a week but I was prepared to sacrifice that because the other features would balance it out. You will have to charge the watch daily.

The watch band is super soft and comfy. I love the interactive bitmoji watch face. I'm still figuring everything out and it took forever to figure out how to get Messenger notifications on the phone. Thank God for YouTube. At first I thought I couldn't use the apps I needed and was ready to return. I'm glad I did more research. All in all I love the look and feel of the watch and love the texting and calling feature. Samsung really needs to work on the battery life because I hate having to charge it everyday but I will learn to live with it for the upgraded features.

title: From a runner's perspective
text:
I bought these headphones specifically for running outside. I had previously purchased in-ear headphones, but found they are quite bad for outside use because when the wind hits them they generate a lot of unwanted noise. I found in-ear headphones are really only good for indoor running. These have no issue with the wind, as the foam earcups resolve that issue. They also connect to my Samsung watch to play Spotify offline, so I can basically just put on these headphones and wear my watch and go for a jog, no need to bring my phone or anything else.

Pros:
- Lightweight, barely really feel them
- Wireless works very smoothly, even with my Samsung watch
- Foam earcups are removable for washing and come with an extra pair
- Amazing price point
- More comfortable than ear buds (at least for me)
- Can hear outside noises like cars etc. for safety

Cons:
- Sound quality will certainly not impress any audiophiles but fine for casual listening

Overall they fit a niche for me. Headphones that can be used for outdoor running that are over the ear, rather than earbuds. I don't know why more headphones don't exist in this category. I also have a child and plan to run with a running stroller. If I used earbuds I might not be able to hear a vehicle etc. These allow you to easily hear ambient noise. I run with them at a lower volume so I can hear basically everything but also enjoy my tunes. I'm really happy with my purchase so far. The only downside is the sound quality. If they had a higher quality sound version with the same build I'd definitely purchase them.

title: Accurate calorie burn! Incorporates heart rate into determining activity calorie burn
text:
I came to Garmin late. I had been a long time Fitbit user and when my Fitbit charge 2 crashed after 2 year's I decided I needed a change and an activity tracking watch that would last longer. I tried both the Fossil Juliana watch and the Samsung Galaxy watch in May 2020 before I tried Garmin. My problem with both the Fossil and Samsung watches Is that even though they both track heart rate, they do not actually incorporate your heart rate into calorie burn during activities. So if you were to take your watch off, Put it down on the table, and start an activity with either the Fossil and the Samsung watch they would give you the exact same calories burned as if you actually had your watch on your wrist and were walking, or if you had your watch on and were running hard. They both have a preset calorie burn per activity level which makes no sense. I think it's ridiculous that they do not incorporate their heart rate tracking into determining the activity calorie burn.

So finally I got to the Garmin venu watch last and I absolutely love it. It tracks heart rate and pulse ox, it incorporates heart rate and intensity level of exercise to give you a more accurate calorie burn, similar to what Fitbit does. I find that the calories burned on the Garmin tend to be a little bit under what I previously got with Fitbit, but Garmin is likely more accurate. I think Fitbit usually overstated calorie burn. I count my daily calorie intake and I find that with my Garmin watch when I have a net zero calorie deficit my weight stays the same, and when I have a calorie deficit i lose weight. So I find it very helpful for weight maintenance. If you have a Fitbit and are thinking of switching to a different brand definitely choose Garmin If you need to watch for activity and calorie tracking. I also like the GPS function for tracking outdoor runs and walks on the Garmin device.

The only thing that is terrible about the Garmin watch is the sleep tracking, it is completely inaccurate and overestimates sleep. Basically as long as you are laying down the watch is going to say you were sleeping and and it says that I get 8 hours of sleep every night, despite the fact that I struggle with insomnia. Fitbit was much more accurate at sleep tracking. The Garmin is also more expensive but I'm hoping it lasts longer than the Fitbit.

title: Functional & VERY basic
text:
Wanted to get this to replace my Samsung 2. Received the new watch and was not expecting the size on this to be so much bigger (See picture for size comparison).
The charger on it is less than appealing. you bump it and it falls off there's no way to secure it, the connection on it is simply magnetic and very easy to dislodge. There are simply for watch faces no more. There is no flashlight and some of the simple things that you get used to with the Samsung feature.
Very little time to set up, very little directions needed and it was Simple and easy for a tech tard like me.
This will hold me over until I can get a new Samsung watch.

title: My favorite Wear OS watch I've ever owned!
text:
Now let me start off real quick with saying that DHL was a big pain to deal with in regards to shipping. They somehow didn't get my correct address so it was stuck in Austin for 3-4 days even after I sent them an address correction. They'd say "It'll be delivered today", but it never came till days later. But on the bright side, it still did come way before was was originally projected as the delivery date which I think was October 13th. All of this just to say be sure to keep track of the shipping once you get your tracking number just in case there's an issue!

But other than that, the experience I've had with this watch has been the absolute best! The battery is way better than the original TicWatch Pro I had and even my Samsung Gear Active that I used to have as well! I've always loved Wear OS but the sluggishness that plagued the original Android Wear watches turned me away. But with updates and newer hardware, it's become soooo much better! I was pretty excited when I heard that the TicWatch Pro 3 was going to have a new and updated processor and it's been great! Like I said the battery life is stellar and I haven't noticed any lag at all. The new backlight added to the dual layer outdoor display is a great addition too, so now I can see it at night. Lastly, the Pro 3 is much lighter than the orignal TicWatch! It's really noticeable when you compare them side by side. Sleeping with it on hasn't been an issue because of how lightweight it is. Speaking of sleeping with it on, the sleep tracking app has worked well too. I use it with the Mobvoi app on my phone so I can see how good or bad my sleep patterns are. The watch even gives recommendations on how to get a better nights sleep based on your own sleep patterns which is nice. I've really enjoyed the TicWatch Pro 3 and I can't recommend it enough!

title: Almost Didn't try them. So Glad I did!!
text:
A quick summary in case you don't want too much info. RUN to the store DON'T WALK and pick these up today!!!!

When I first received this unit I found that I did not like the color at all. Grey is the wrong shade for headphones like these. But forgetting about the color lets get into the whole device itself!!

The Good!

The headphones fit great! I love that they are not as uncomfortable as you would think they look. In case they current inserts are too small or too big there is 1 smaller and 1 larger size ear and lobe insert.

Battery life is amazing on the headsets for music or streaming I do wish it was a bit longer for phone calls.

Moving and pairing the unit between phone and PC is usually a hassle with Bluetooth devices but these moved between seamlessly without any issues. I LOVE THIS!!!!

The other thing that is amazing is that you can pass the 1 of the headphones to a friend, partner, Significant other, child, etc. and you can both listen in without worry of wires!!

USB-C charging so quick charge up with appropriate charging brick.

The headphones have some onboard memory as well so you can put songs on it for workouts or just a playlist to zone out to. It is less than 4 gigs but enough to get a good list of songs to listen to for a while. Nice surprise but not needed as I stream directly from my phone/PC.

Now the bad.

As I stated before I wish the grey was a different color grey more charcoal or just a darker shade in general.

You can only charge the headphones in the charging case. With wireless charging an option it should have been used instead,

As per charging there is no charging brick so you need to have one and make sure it is newer to otherwise it will be a slow charge.

Have to use Samsung Gear app for initial setup. I don't like being locked to software but at least it checks for and perform firmware updates.

Voice commands on the unit are not always heard and if sweating it will affect the touch controls. It causes them to react incorrectly not all the time but often enough to be a small annoyance.

Last but not least the sound quality is not amazing. Even though I say this I still think they sound great for the type of headphone they are.

So in closing:

Pros:

Fits well!
Battery Life - Music/streaming.
Paring and switching devices a breeze.
Share what you hear!
Onboard storage
USB-C Charging

Cons:

Bad color
Case only charging
No Charging brick
Must use app
Some touch issues if wet/sweating
Sound quality, not 100%

title: Good, not great (not worth $200 and upgrading from the original Vivoactive)
text:
Started with a Vivoactive, went to Samsung Galaxy Watch (returned due to lack of fitness ability), went to Vivoactive 3, returned and kept my original.

The Greats:
- SO comfortable! That band is so nice.
- Good fitness ability
- Intuitive side scroll and face scroll (Some on here wrote the side scroll was USELess and claimed to keep accidentally hitting it, they must have had some fat wrists....)
- Easy to use
- Customizable
- SUP/Kayaking mode is sweet

The Not so Greats:
- The HR monitor is not accurate - compared side by side to chest rate monitor and Vivoactive original - the Vivo 3 was about 20% off on Avg HR, and Max HR and 30-40% off on calorie burn - kind of useless
- Battery life is more like 3 days, not 5 (no GPS use)
- No music able to be downloaded onto it
- Display does not come on well with wrist movement

It's a GOOD fitness watch that tries to be smart (exactly how I described my original Vivoactive), but I am returning it as I just don't think the added benefits over my Vivoactive watch are there (24/7 HR monitoring - who cares! it kills battery / Some added workouts - eh / Music control - the new update on my Vivoactive now allows this / Paying with the watch - meh, I don't think that's that useful).

If this watch accurately recorded HR during a workout and got a SOLID 4-5 days of battery, I would keep it and retire my original Vivoactive ... but the price tag does not warrant it IMO.

title: You can't go wrong with this watch
text:
I got this watch to replace my Samsung Galaxy Watch, the main reason was the battery, I was so tired of having to charge my watch every day, I had to install TWO applications in my phone to have my watch working, besides, Samsung was trying to shove down my throat the Samsung Health, Samsung Pay and Bixby, long story short, I just throw it away, it sucked.

This watch was "cheaper", I got a solid 20 days of battery, it tracks what I need and no more, sleep, hearth rate, steps and notifications, the watchfaces aren't that much but are good enough, you might get more from an expensier device but this watch is the perfect balance between features and price, the design is sleek and simple, light and it offers exactly what I was looking for.

title: Huawei vs Samsung Gear S2
text:
I just sold my Samsung Gear S2 and purchased this watch. Wow! Night and Day!

1. Samsung OS (Tizen) is crap: few apps, not intuitive, not customizable. Huawei uses Android wear which is not perfect, but much more intuitive
2. Huawei's implementation of the features are logical and useful: ie notifications, receiving and replying to texts, etc... Samsung's...not.
3. Bigger screen on the Huawei means I can see the text!
4. Packaging is amazing on the Huawei. Feels like a Rolex.
5. Samsung band is cheap - Huawei is high quality leather
6. Samsung forces you to use the "S Health" fitness app which is crap. With Huawei you can use Google Fit.
7. Gear S2 has lots of Bloatware that you can't remove.

There is no comparison between these watches. Get the Huawei...period.

title: Do you REALLY need more?
text:
While my Citizen does a great job of keeping time and has solar to power it, it does not do a great job with the date. It only knows 31 days for each month, so I'd have to adjust it every month with 30 days. Eventually I just stopped adjusting it. Now THIS watch, while not the levels of an Apple or Samsung smart watches, is more of a "bright" watch. It has what I NEED in a watch, shows 24h time, display time AND day correctly, never have to adjust for day light saving time, great battery, good build, good looking, show weather, show notifications from phone, allow for multiple faces or I can import a background and place the time in a couple of places.
I also bought this on sale since I thought it might be a toy but turns out it is not. It also comes with an additional screen protector and proprietary cable. The watch is very light and has very good battery life. It does have some limitations such as you get a limited number of faces, and most are meh. While I like that I can import a picture for a customized background, you can't change the font styles and the color options are limited, and you are limited to where you can position the time. The watch has other features, like stopwatch, steps counter, count down time, but I rarely use those. I like the shake to wake up feature, but it works when it wants to, not every time. I like the rugged look and it is sturdy, after wearing it until now, I don't see any scratching other than on the screen protector and when you see the watch, you really don't notice the scratching. I really like this watch and am thinking about turning in my more expensive one. You do need to install the Da Fit app on your phone to pair it to the watch. Overall, I'm very happy with this watch.

title: Honest review
text:
Firstly, this is an honest review by an actual customer. The watch is solid, heavy, and feels premium. Apps are the standard stuff. The keyboard is useless and there is no option for a qwerty keyboard. I did read that if your willing to jump thru hoops, you can install a Google based keyboard. (I tried and it's froze watch, so i factory reset). Not much in way of downloadable apps. Battery life is a solid 2 days.
Now on to health app. Primarily I bought it for sleep tracking so I could move my fitbit charge 3 to my ankle for accurate step count. The sleep app on fitbit is considerably more accurate than the Samsung Watch 3. Same with steps count. It would be nice to be able to upload fitbit step count to Samsung, but it's not possible. You can only manually add distance. (Anyone that says otherwise is not being honest).
The main point I dislike about the watch is the band clasp (I have the titanium watch). The clasp doesn't stay closed and I must snap it closed several times a day or risk dropping the watch. I don't wear sleeves or anything to catch on it to pull open. I've even made it loose on my wrist thinking the way I bent my hand to wrist was causing it, but it wasn't. It's a poorly made clasp.

title: You Lose Most Functionality in Essential Mode
text:
I like the watch, but I find the Essential Mode (Battery Saving) to be worthless. I got used to my Samsung watches in battery saving mode, which allowed the alarm to vibrate or ring while in battery saving mode. I sleep with my watch on and would like to have it in Battery Saving (Essential Mode) since I don't use it while sleeping, but I still want it to wake me.
This watch doesn't do that. Big loss. Now I have to use my alarm clock, which wakes my wife, too.

title: No Flaws
text:
I am a professional photographer. I received this as a promo when I purchased my Galaxy Note. I do not get paid or compensated for this review. I have read a couple of reviews and they are misleading. First off, the software for Windows/Mac is NOT hard to find. It took me 5 seconds to find it. Just Google "Samsung Gear 360 Camera" choose the official Samsung site. It takes you straight to the Gear's product site and immediately to the right in the grey box it says "Requires MicroSD memory card to capture and save content.
For Windows/Mac software, click here "
NOT HARD.
Also top heavy? Maybe if you put it on an uneven surface. I was on top of the Arch de Triumph, put this (without any artificial support), on a piece of the original Arch outside of the fenced area on top of it in windy conditions, took many photos without so much as touching it. It did not fall. In fact, using my phone as a remote utilizing live view on my phone (AWESOME) I was able to control the iso and speed settings, and captured some wonderful photos (regular stills, 180s and 360s)!!!! This thing is AWESOME!!! NO Flaws, so long as you are able to use simple tech. I mean it's 2018, everyone should know how to use this simple tech by know, or at least be able to download the software that is made available easily.

Get outside and take some photos!!!! So much history, so much nature to see!!!!

title: Not the greatest smart home tool, and probably won't be for a while
text:
I got this because the Pixel watch isn't coming out for a while *tears*. So far the Samsung watch is...ok (keep reading).

The design is great, even on my tiny tiny rist. I wish it was charged right out of the box.

The apps & widgets are a challenge!! There are very few good apps available in the store. It's REALLY hard to find a 4 star app. Gmail, Google Maps, Netflix, or Nest apps are not available yet. There's a Google assistant patch (GAssist.net), but you have to be somewhat tech savvy to install it. There is a 3rd party $2 Alexa assistant app. Be prepared to spend money on good-ish apps. This watch isn't going to be a great smart home tool right out of the box, and probably won't be perfect for quite some time.

title: Great activity tracker but not a great battery life
text: I was so excited to get this activity tracker since I just got the Samsung Galaxy S7 phone. The Gear Fit2 also sends notifications, texts, track the weather. When I received the Samsung Gear Fit2 the battery was at 15% and took 1 1/2 hours to fully charged. I was able to get a interactive face for it in the app store for free but realized that my battery was dead within a day. The recommended battery life is 3-4 days and mine last 2 days max with the brightness on the lowest and the do not disturb on. I do not feel like it tracks my steps accurate. I like that I can track the weather, water intake, coffee intake, alarm, time walking/running. I gave it 4 out of 5 stars because of the battery life and step accuracy. Besides those 2 things, the Samsung Gear Fit2 is Great! #insidersgearfit2

title: Works as expected and less expensive than most of the competition
text: My wife wore a Fitbit for about a year. The bands would only last a few months and then we had to struggle to get the band release to work to install a replacement. The plastic finally broke to the point that a new band couldn't be installed. About the same time the screen stopped working. The whole thing gave the impression that it was a throwaway device. She then moved to a Samsung Watch which synced to her Samsung phone. I was interested in something that had a combination of the features of the two but at a reasonable cost. This watch fits that niche perfectly. It's constructed much better than the Fitbit having a metal case with easily swappable standard bands. The battery lasts a whole week where my wife needs to charge her Samsung watch every other day. The Runmefit app that pairs with it has most of the features that similar apps have and syncs easily with my Samsung phone. I work in a hospital and was surprised to see how closely the blood oxygen and blood pressure indicated by the watch matches those taken by dedicated medical equipment. I'm pretty tough on watches but this is holding up well. There are still no scratches on the screen and the band is as good as new. I may replace the included rubber band with a cloth or leather strap but that is easy to do as it's a standard 22mm band.

title: Was skeptical about a inexpensive watch but they proved me wrong
text:
My first attempt to buy a smart watch was years ago as a samsung watch and there were just to many issues. It was bulky and batter life as maybe 2 days. So I went back to a real watch because that's all I really needed. Fast forward to 2022 and Black Friday. I picked up the Amazfit Bip 3 Pro for around 50.00.

My first impression was it was a good size. My wrists are small and it was in the size range I wanted. It is very light with the included band and a little heavier with my replacement SS band. Really in the range of any watch though.

My second thought was on the band. It was a rubberized substance which I really hate. I used it for about a week and decided it was not for me. I was worried but this uses a standard size and there are a lot of bands out there that will fit this on Amazon. I ended up getting a stainless steel replacement for 10.00. and just love it.

The Battery life is spot on their estimate. It claimed 14 days. I am past 14 days at 19% power. To me this is good. You can charge it every 2 weeks and not be worried you will run out towards the end.

The movement to turn on seems well done. It comes on when you move your wrist to view but really does not come on by just moving your wrist or arm. You will need to remember to turn this feature off at night. You can set times when it is active. I have mine set from 7AM to 11PM and if I need it after that just hit the button. If you don't it will come on when you don't want it to and its bright.

There are a minimal number of clock faces. It would be nice if there were more. I actually use the default and have one standard watch face if I want to look fancier.

You can get text and email notifications but they are not the best. You cannot reply to anything though.

You can answer calls but there is not mic or speaker so it really does you know good. Im not sure I want to look like Dick Tracey and talk into my arm.

I don't really use the measuring features as I really just wanted a watch. I have played with them and they seem to be right but I really have no way to tell that.

I bought this as a watch and the battery life and screen resolution make this a good choice.

Overall I bought this

title: Great Watch, Average App
text:
I have a Samsung Gear S3 smartwatch but have been disappointed with it's performance as a fitness watch. The heart rate monitor always reads faster than my actual heart rate, distances don't seem spot on, and pace figures fluctuate wildly when I run. Got this ProSense 307 and it works more accurately. The heart rate readings are always accurate, the pace doesn't swing so much, and distance figures are precise. The battery life is also much better with the Epson. My Gear S3 needs a charge every 2 days at best, but the Epson can go twice that long easily. I also love the charging clip - it's smaller than the traditional cradle chargers used by Samsung and on previous Epson multisport watches and very portable. The Epson isn't quite as easy to navigate since it lacks a touch screen, but it's pretty easy to learn and overall the watch itself is great.

What keeps the watch from being a 5 star item is the Epson View software. Getting it up and running was a pain because the app kept giving me a cryptic warning whenever I ran it (something about going to settings and allowing automatic background updates, which wasn't even an option in my phone's settings). After much consternation and frustration, I finally figured out that what the app really wanted was for me to turn on my phone's gps during the app's setup process. Once I finally go it going, it syncs easily with the watch, but the app itself is a little schizophrenic. It doesn't quite have the polish and feng shui you'd expect from an Android app in 2018 (it feels like an app that's 8 or 10 years old). It's not terrible, but not as easy to navigate and find helpful info as it should be.

In the end, I like the watch a lot and it makes a great fitness tool. It also looks nice in a sporty, low key kind of way. It features great accuracy from the heart monitor and gps and is pretty easy to navigate once you get used to it. If it had a better app, I'd give it 5 stars. Until Epson reboots the app, however, the whole experience isn't all it could be. Great watch, average app.

text:
**update: one day after I wrote this, the left earbud stopped working and none of the troubleshooting steps worked so I'm reducing from 5 to 4 stars**

For the price, I really don't think you can beat these. I've been using them for over 6 months, and I'm so pleased with the fit, sound quality, and battery life. I used
them for running, walking, around the house, in, around the house, and can even wear them to sleep. I love the charging case, and various bud sizes to get the right fit. It's been hard for me to find earbuds that I can use while running running, and these give me no problems. My only complaint is that they are very touch sensitive so it is easy to accidentally pause what you're listening to, but seems less easy to hit play to get it going again.. Very minor issue.. These pair well with everything from my phone, to my Bluetooth speaker any Samsung watch.
